question: What is Angular and how is it different from AngularJS?
answer:
Angular (v2+) is a TypeScript-based, component-driven SPA framework by Google. It ships a full platform: components, dependency injection, routing, forms, HTTP, and RxJS.

AngularJS (v1.x) was JavaScript-based and used controllers, scopes, and two-way digest-cycle binding. Angular is a complete rewrite: component architecture, a hierarchical injector, AOT compilation, and far better performance.
tip: Say "Angular" for 2+ and "AngularJS" only for 1.x — interviewers notice the distinction.

question: Lifecycle hooks — order and what each is for.
answer:
Order: constructor → ngOnChanges → ngOnInit → ngDoCheck → ngAfterContentInit → ngAfterContentChecked → ngAfterViewInit → ngAfterViewChecked → ngOnDestroy.

  • ngOnChanges — runs when an @Input changes (receives SimpleChanges).
  • ngOnInit — one-time init; fetch data here, not in the constructor.
  • ngAfterViewInit — view and @ViewChild refs are ready.
  • ngOnDestroy — cleanup: unsubscribe, clear timers.
tip: The constructor is for dependency injection only; the DOM and inputs aren't ready yet — do real work in ngOnInit.
deep:
The full sequence & how often each runs

HookWhenHow often
constructorclass instantiated (DI only)once
ngOnChangesa bound @Input changesevery input change
ngOnInitafter first ngOnChanges — inputs setonce
ngDoCheckevery change-detection runfrequently
ngAfterContentInitprojected content (ng-content) readyonce
ngAfterContentCheckedafter projected content checkedfrequently
ngAfterViewInitcomponent's view + child views readyonce
ngAfterViewCheckedafter the view is checkedfrequently
ngOnDestroyjust before the component is destroyedonce

Why not just use the constructor?

The constructor runs before Angular has wired up inputs or rendered the view. At that point @Input values are still undefined and @ViewChild refs don't exist. So the constructor is only for dependency injection; put initialization logic (data fetching, setup that needs inputs) in ngOnInit.

ngAfterViewInit & @ViewChild timing

ngAfterViewInit fires once, after the component's template (and its children's views) are rendered in the DOM. This is the earliest point a @ViewChild / @ViewChildren reference is reliably available — e.g. to init a chart or measure an element.

If a @ViewChild is inside an *ngIf/@if, it resolves later — use { static: false } (the default) and read it in ngAfterViewInit, not ngOnInit.

ngOnDestroy — the cleanup hook

Runs once, right before Angular destroys the component. It's crucial for preventing memory leaks. Use it to:

  • Unsubscribe from long-lived RxJS streams (Subjects, valueChanges, intervals).
  • Detach manual event listeners you added.
  • Clear setTimeout/setInterval timers.
private destroy$ = new Subject<void>();

ngOnInit() {
  this.data$.pipe(takeUntil(this.destroy$)).subscribe(...);
}
ngOnDestroy() {
  this.destroy$.next();
  this.destroy$.complete();
}

(HTTP calls complete on their own, so they don't strictly need cleanup — but streams that never complete do.)

Common pitfalls

  • Doing DOM work in ngOnInit — the view isn't ready; use ngAfterViewInit.
  • Heavy logic in ngDoCheck/…Checked hooks — they run on every cycle and will tank performance.
  • Forgetting ngOnDestroy cleanup — the #1 source of Angular memory leaks.
  • Updating a value in ngAfterViewInit that's shown in the same view → ExpressionChangedAfterItHasBeenChecked error (defer with a microtask or move to ngOnInit).

How to say it in an interview

“The constructor is only for DI. I fetch data in ngOnInit once inputs are set, react to input changes in ngOnChanges, touch the rendered DOM / @ViewChild in ngAfterViewInit, and always clean up subscriptions and timers in ngOnDestroy to avoid leaks.”

question: What are the types of data binding?
answer:
• Interpolation — {{ value }} (component → view).
  • Property binding — [src]="url" (component → view).
  • Event binding — (click)="fn()" (view → component).
  • Two-way binding — [(ngModel)]="name" (both directions; the "banana in a box").
tip: Two-way binding is just property + event binding combined under the hood.
deep:
The four bindings and their direction

BindingSyntaxDirection
Interpolation{{ value }}class → view
Property[prop]="value"class → view
Event(event)="fn($event)"view → class
Two-way[(ngModel)]="value"both

Interpolation vs property binding

Interpolation is a string shortcut — {{ x }} is coerced to text. Property binding sets the actual DOM property with the evaluated type, so use it whenever the value isn't a string:

<!-- string attribute -->
<img src="{{ url }}">
<!-- real property, keeps type -->
<img [src]="url">
<input [disabled]="isLocked">  <!-- boolean, not the string "false" -->

A common bug: disabled="{{ false }}" renders disabled="false" which the DOM still treats as disabled. [disabled]="false" works correctly.

How two-way binding really works

The "banana in a box" [()] is pure syntax sugar for a property binding + an event binding. Angular expands:

<input [(ngModel)]="name">
<!-- is exactly -->
<input [ngModel]="name" (ngModelChange)="name = $event">

Any input paired with an output named <input>Change becomes two-way bindable — that's how you build your own (see the custom two-way binding question).

Attribute vs property binding

Most bindings target DOM properties. A few things only exist as HTML attributes (ARIA, colspan, SVG) — for those use [attr.aria-label]="...". Similar helpers: [class.active], [style.width.px].

How to say it in an interview

“There are four: interpolation and property binding push data to the view, event binding sends events back, and two-way binding combines a property and an event binding — it’s literally [x] plus (xChange) under the hood.”

question: What is the Change Detection (CD) Tree in Angular?
answer:
The Change Detection (CD) Tree is Angular's internal tree of component views. Every component becomes a node in this tree. During change detection, Angular starts at the root component and traverses the tree, checking each component for data changes and updating the DOM when necessary.

Components using the OnPush strategy can skip change detection unless they are explicitly marked for checking or one of their inputs changes, making the CD tree traversal more efficient.
tip: Think of the CD Tree as the component hierarchy that Angular walks during every change detection cycle.
code:
AppComponent
├── HeaderComponent
├── DashboardComponent
│   ├── UserCardComponent
│   └── ChartComponent
└── FooterComponent
deep:
What is the CD Tree?

Angular represents the application's component hierarchy as a Change Detection (CD) Tree. Each component is a node, and the root component is at the top of the tree.

AppComponent
├── HeaderComponent
├── DashboardComponent
│   ├── UserCardComponent
│   └── ChartComponent
└── FooterComponent

How Change Detection Works

  1. An event occurs (click, HTTP response, timer, etc.).
  2. Angular starts change detection from the root component.
  3. It traverses the CD Tree from parent to child.
  4. Each component's bindings are checked.
  5. If a value has changed, Angular updates the DOM.

OnPush and the CD Tree

With the default strategy, Angular checks every component in the tree. Components using ChangeDetectionStrategy.OnPush are skipped unless:

  • An @Input reference changes.
  • An event occurs inside the component.
  • An observable emits using the async pipe.
  • markForCheck() is called.

This reduces unnecessary work and improves performance.

Why is the CD Tree Important?

  • Explains how Angular updates the UI.
  • Helps optimize performance.
  • Makes it easier to understand OnPush.
  • Useful for debugging unnecessary change detection.

Common Interview Questions

  • What is the CD Tree? Angular's internal tree of component views used during change detection.
  • Where does change detection start? From the root component.
  • In which direction is the tree traversed? From parent to child.
  • How does OnPush affect the CD Tree? It skips checking components unless specific conditions trigger them.

How to say it in an interview

"The Change Detection Tree is Angular's internal representation of the component hierarchy. During every change detection cycle, Angular traverses this tree from the root component, checking each component for changes. Components using the OnPush strategy can be skipped unless their inputs change, an event occurs, an observable emits through the async pipe, or they are manually marked for checking, which improves application performance."

question: What are the types of directives?
answer:
• Component — a directive with a template (the most common).
  • Structural — change DOM layout: *ngIf, *ngFor, *ngSwitch (v17+: @if, @for).
  • Attribute — change appearance/behaviour: ngClass, ngStyle, or your own.
tip: The leading * on structural directives is sugar for an <ng-template> wrapper.

question: How do components communicate with each other?
answer:
• Parent → child: @Input().
  • Child → parent: @Output() with an EventEmitter.
  • Unrelated components: a shared service with a BehaviorSubject (or a signal / NgRx store).
  • Parent accessing child: @ViewChild.
tip: For siblings/unrelated components, prefer a shared service over chaining inputs and outputs.
code:
@Output() saved = new EventEmitter<Item>();
onSave() { this.saved.emit(this.item); }

question: Explain Dependency Injection (DI) and providedIn: 'root' in Angular.
answer:
Dependency Injection (DI) is a design pattern where Angular automatically creates and provides the objects (dependencies) a class needs instead of the class creating them itself. Angular uses a hierarchical injector to manage these dependencies.

@Injectable({ providedIn: 'root' }) registers a service in the application's root injector, making it a singleton shared across the entire application. It is also tree-shakable, so unused services are removed from production builds.
tip: Use providedIn: 'root' for most services. Provide a service at the component level only when each component should have its own instance.
deep:
What is Dependency Injection?

Dependency Injection (DI) is a design pattern in which an object receives the dependencies it needs from an external source instead of creating them itself.

Instead of writing:

const service = new UserService();

Angular automatically creates and injects the service.

constructor(private userService: UserService) {}

This makes applications more modular, reusable and easier to test.

Why use Dependency Injection?

  • Reduces tight coupling.
  • Improves testability.
  • Promotes code reuse.
  • Centralizes object creation.
  • Makes dependency management easier.

How Angular DI Works

  1. A component requests a dependency.
  2. Angular looks in the current injector.
  3. If not found, Angular searches parent injectors.
  4. If found, Angular returns the instance.
  5. If not found anywhere, Angular throws a NullInjectorError.

Hierarchical Injector

Angular uses a hierarchy of injectors.

Root Injector
    │
Feature Injector
    │
Component Injector

A child injector first checks itself before searching its parent.

What does providedIn: 'root' mean?

@Injectable({
  providedIn: 'root'
})
export class UserService {}

This registers the service in the root injector.

Advantages:

  • Singleton instance.
  • Available throughout the application.
  • Tree-shakable.
  • No need to add it to a providers array.

Singleton Example

Both components receive the same instance.

@Component({...})
export class HomeComponent {
  constructor(public service: UserService) {}
}

@Component({...})
export class DashboardComponent {
  constructor(public service: UserService) {}
}

Updating data in one component is visible in the other because both share the same service instance.

Component-level Provider

@Component({
  providers: [UserService]
})
export class HomeComponent {}

Each HomeComponent instance receives its own UserService.

This is useful when service state should not be shared.

Root vs Component Provider

providedIn: 'root'Component providers
SingletonNew instance per component
Shared stateIndependent state
Application-wideComponent scope
Tree-shakableNot tree-shakable

The inject() Function

Angular also supports the inject() function.

const userService = inject(UserService);

It is commonly used in standalone APIs, functional guards, interceptors and effects.

Real-world Angular Example

  • UserService
  • AuthService
  • HttpClient
  • Router
  • ActivatedRoute
  • MatDialog

All of these are obtained using Dependency Injection.

Common Interview Questions

  • What is Dependency Injection? A design pattern where Angular provides required dependencies instead of creating them manually.
  • What is an Injector? An object responsible for creating and supplying dependencies.
  • What does providedIn: 'root' do? Registers the service in the root injector as a singleton.
  • Why is providedIn preferred over providers? It supports tree-shaking and avoids unnecessary module configuration.
  • When would you use component providers? When each component should have its own service instance.
  • What happens if Angular cannot resolve a dependency? It throws NullInjectorError.
  • What is the difference between constructor injection and inject()? Constructor injection is the traditional approach, while inject() is useful outside constructors and in functional APIs.

How to say it in an interview

"Dependency Injection is a design pattern where Angular automatically creates and provides the dependencies a class needs. Angular manages dependencies through a hierarchical injector. Using providedIn: 'root' registers a service in the root injector, creating a singleton shared across the application while keeping it tree-shakable. If a service is provided at the component level, each component gets its own instance instead."

question: Template-driven vs Reactive forms — which and why?
answer:
Template-driven — logic lives in the template via ngModel; quick, good for simple forms.

Reactive — the form model is defined in the TS class (FormGroup, FormControl, FormBuilder); explicit, testable, and better for complex or dynamic validation.
tip: For anything non-trivial, reach for Reactive forms — they're easier to unit test.
deep:
Side-by-side

Template-drivenReactive
Model lives inthe template (ngModel)the TS class (FormGroup)
ModuleFormsModuleReactiveFormsModule
Data flowasync (two-way)synchronous & explicit
Validationdirectives in templatefunctions in class
Dynamic controlshardeasy (FormArray)
Unit testingneeds the DOMtest the model directly
Best forsimple, small formscomplex / dynamic forms

Template-driven — quick and declarative

<form #f="ngForm" (ngSubmit)="save(f.value)">
  <input name="email" ngModel required email>
</form>

Angular builds the FormGroup for you behind the scenes, one FormControl per ngModel. Validation is added as attribute directives.

Reactive — explicit and testable

form = this.fb.group({
  email: ['', [Validators.required, Validators.email]],
  password: ['', Validators.minLength(8)],
});

save() {
  if (this.form.invalid) return;
  this.api.login(this.form.value);
}

You hold the source of truth. You can valueChanges.pipe(debounceTime(300)), add/remove controls at runtime, and unit-test without rendering anything.

Which and why (the answer they want)

“I default to Reactive forms for anything beyond a trivial form: the model is in TypeScript so it’s type-safe, testable, and supports dynamic controls and reactive validation. I only use template-driven for a quick, small form like a single search box.”

Common pitfalls

  • Mixing ngModel with a reactive formControlName on the same field — pick one.
  • Forgetting to import the right module (ReactiveFormsModule vs FormsModule).
  • Reading form.value and expecting disabled controls — they’re excluded; use getRawValue().

question: Explain routing: lazy loading, guards, and resolvers.
answer:
• Lazy loading — load a feature only when visited via loadChildren / loadComponent. Smaller initial bundle, faster first paint.
  • Guards — CanActivate (auth), CanDeactivate (unsaved-changes prompt), CanMatch.
  • Resolver — pre-fetch data before the route activates so the component opens with data ready.
tip: Lazy loading is the single biggest lever for initial load performance in large apps.
code:
{ path: 'admin', loadComponent: () =>
  import('./admin.component').then(m => m.AdminComponent) }
deep:
Routing in Angular

Angular Router enables navigation between different views without reloading the page. A route maps a URL to a component. In large applications, routing is commonly combined with lazy loading, route guards, and resolvers to improve performance, security, and user experience.

1. Lazy Loading

Lazy loading means a feature is downloaded only when the user navigates to it instead of being included in the initial bundle. This reduces the application's initial download size and improves load time.

const routes: Routes = [
  {
    path: 'admin',
    loadChildren: () =>
      import('./admin/admin.routes').then(m => m.ADMIN_ROUTES)
  }
];

Example: An e-commerce application has Customer, Admin, Reports, and Analytics modules. Most users never visit Admin, so downloading it only when required significantly improves startup performance.

2. Route Guards

Guards decide whether navigation should be allowed. They are commonly used for authentication, authorization, preventing data loss, and conditional routing.

  • CanActivate – Prevents access to a route if the user is not authorized.
  • CanDeactivate – Prevents leaving a page with unsaved changes.
  • CanMatch – Determines whether a route should match before loading it.
  • CanActivateChild – Protects child routes.
export const authGuard: CanActivateFn = () => {
  return inject(AuthService).isLoggedIn();
};

{
  path: 'admin',
  canActivate: [authGuard],
  loadComponent: () => import('./admin.component')
    .then(m => m.AdminComponent)
}

Real-world example: In a banking application, if the user is not logged in and tries to open /accounts, the guard redirects them to the login page.

3. Resolver

A resolver fetches required data before the component is created. This ensures the page opens with data already available instead of showing loading spinners inside the component.

export const userResolver: ResolveFn<User> = (route) => {
  return inject(UserService).getUser(route.params['id']);
};

{
  path: 'users/:id',
  component: UserComponent,
  resolve: {
    user: userResolver
  }
}
export class UserComponent {
  user = inject(ActivatedRoute).snapshot.data['user'];
}

Real-world example: When opening a customer profile, the customer's details are loaded before the page is displayed, avoiding an empty screen followed by a loading spinner.

Putting them together

{
  path: 'admin',
  canActivate: [authGuard],
  resolve: {
    settings: settingsResolver
  },
  loadComponent: () =>
    import('./admin.component').then(m => m.AdminComponent)
}

Navigation flow:

  1. User navigates to /admin.
  2. Angular lazy-loads the Admin feature.
  3. The authentication guard checks whether the user is logged in.
  4. If allowed, the resolver fetches required settings or user data.
  5. Finally, the Admin component is displayed with all required data.

Interview Tip

Remember the sequence: Lazy Loading → Guard → Resolver → Component. Explain that lazy loading improves performance, guards protect routes, and resolvers ensure data is ready before rendering. This is the architecture commonly used in enterprise Angular applications.

question: What are HTTP interceptors used for?
answer: HTTP Interceptors are middleware that intercept every outgoing HTTP request and incoming HTTP response in an Angular application. They allow you to add, modify, or handle requests and responses globally without changing individual service methods. Common uses include attaching JWT tokens, adding custom headers, logging, displaying global loaders, handling errors, retrying failed requests, and refreshing expired access tokens.
tip: Think of an interceptor as Angular's middleware. Every request passes through it before reaching the server, and every response passes through it before reaching your application.
deep:
What is an HTTP Interceptor?

An HTTP Interceptor is a service that sits between your Angular application and the backend server. Every outgoing request and incoming response automatically passes through the interceptor.

Component
      ↓
UserService
      ↓
HttpClient
      ↓
HTTP Interceptor
      ↓
Backend API
      ↑
HTTP Response
      ↑
HTTP Interceptor
      ↑
Component

This allows common logic to be implemented once instead of repeating it in every API call.

Common Uses

  • Attach JWT or OAuth access tokens.
  • Add common HTTP headers.
  • Display a global loading spinner.
  • Log requests and responses.
  • Handle HTTP errors globally.
  • Refresh expired access tokens.
  • Retry failed requests.
  • Measure request duration.

Adding Authentication Token

intercept(req: HttpRequest<any>, next: HttpHandler) {
  const token = localStorage.getItem('token');

  const authReq = req.clone({
    setHeaders: {
      Authorization: `Bearer ${token}`
    }
  });

  return next.handle(authReq);
}

Since HttpRequest is immutable, you must call clone() before modifying it.

Global Error Handling

return next.handle(req).pipe(
  catchError((error: HttpErrorResponse) => {
    if (error.status === 401) {
      console.log('Unauthorized');
    }

    if (error.status === 500) {
      console.log('Server Error');
    }

    return throwError(() => error);
  })
);

Instead of writing catchError() in every service, one interceptor can handle errors for the entire application.

Loading Spinner

intercept(req: HttpRequest<any>, next: HttpHandler) {
  this.loader.show();

  return next.handle(req).pipe(
    finalize(() => this.loader.hide())
  );
}

The loader appears before the request and disappears automatically when the request finishes.

Refresh Token Flow

User Request
      ↓
Access Token Expired
      ↓
401 Unauthorized
      ↓
Interceptor
      ↓
Refresh Token API
      ↓
New Access Token
      ↓
Retry Original Request

This is one of the most common enterprise use cases for HTTP Interceptors.

Functional Interceptors (Angular 15+)

Modern Angular supports functional interceptors using HttpInterceptorFn.

export const authInterceptor: HttpInterceptorFn = (req, next) => {
  const token = localStorage.getItem('token');

  return next(
    req.clone({
      setHeaders: {
        Authorization: `Bearer ${token}`
      }
    })
  );
};

Functional interceptors are tree-shakable and are the recommended approach in standalone Angular applications.

Registering an Interceptor

Standalone Angular (Recommended)

bootstrapApplication(AppComponent, {
  providers: [
    provideHttpClient(
      withInterceptors([authInterceptor])
    )
  ]
});

NgModule

providers: [
  {
    provide: HTTP_INTERCEPTORS,
    useClass: AuthInterceptor,
    multi: true
  }
]

Execution Order

If multiple interceptors are registered, requests flow in the order they are provided, while responses flow back in the reverse order.

Request
Auth Interceptor
      ↓
Loader Interceptor
      ↓
Logging Interceptor
      ↓
Backend
      ↑
Logging
      ↑
Loader
      ↑
Auth

This ordering is important when combining authentication, logging, and loading indicators.

Real-world Enterprise Example

In most enterprise Angular applications, a request follows this pipeline:

  1. Attach JWT token.
  2. Show loading spinner.
  3. Send request.
  4. Handle errors globally.
  5. If 401 occurs, refresh the token.
  6. Retry the original request.
  7. Hide the loader.

This keeps services focused only on business logic while cross-cutting concerns are handled centrally.

Interview Tip

Interviewers commonly ask 'Why use an interceptor instead of adding headers inside every service?' The answer is that interceptors centralize common logic, reduce code duplication, and make maintenance easier. Also mention that HttpRequest objects are immutable, so you must always use clone() before modifying a request.

Interview Answer

"HTTP Interceptors act as middleware for Angular's HttpClient. Every request and response passes through them, allowing developers to implement cross-cutting concerns such as authentication, adding headers, logging, global loading indicators, error handling, token refresh, and retry logic in one place. Requests are immutable, so they must be cloned before modification, and interceptors execute in the order they are registered."

question: Explain content projection (ng-content) and ViewChild/ContentChild.
answer:
ng-content projects markup that a parent passes into a child (like slots) — key for reusable UI such as cards and modals.

@ViewChild references an element/component in this template; @ContentChild references projected content. They're ready in ngAfterViewInit and ngAfterContentInit respectively.
tip: Use multi-slot projection with select: <ng-content select="[header]">.
deep:
Content projection = slots

ng-content lets a parent pass markup into a child, so the child owns the shell and the parent owns the content. This is how you build reusable <app-card>, modals, and layout wrappers.

<!-- card.component.html -->
<div class="card">
  <header><ng-content select="[card-title]"></ng-content></header>
  <div class="body"><ng-content></ng-content></div>
</div>

<!-- usage -->
<app-card>
  <h2 card-title>Title</h2>
  <p>Any body markup here.</p>
</app-card>

The first selected slot catches matching elements; the plain <ng-content> catches everything else.

ViewChild vs ContentChild — the key difference

@ViewChild@ContentChild
Querieselements in this component's own templateelements projected in via ng-content
Ready inngAfterViewInitngAfterContentInit

Mnemonic: View = what you wrote in your template; Content = what someone else handed you through the slot.

@ViewChild('input') input!: ElementRef;      // in my template
@ContentChild(TabComponent) tab!: TabComponent; // projected in

ngAfterViewInit()    { this.input.nativeElement.focus(); }
ngAfterContentInit() { /* this.tab is available here */ }

Why the timing matters

Reading a @ViewChild in ngOnInit often returns undefined because the view isn't rendered yet — read it in ngAfterViewInit. Same for @ContentChild and ngAfterContentInit. (Signal queries in v17+ remove much of this timing pain.)

How to say it in an interview

“ng-content projects a parent’s markup into a reusable child, like slots. @ViewChild grabs something from my own template and is ready in ngAfterViewInit; @ContentChild grabs projected content and is ready in ngAfterContentInit.”

question: How does change detection work? Default vs OnPush.
answer:
zone.js patches async APIs and tells Angular to re-check the component tree after events. The Default strategy checks every component each cycle.

OnPush only re-checks a component when: an @Input reference changes, an event fires inside it, or an async pipe emits. Combined with immutable data it drastically cuts re-renders. (Signals push toward zone-less change detection.)
tip: OnPush + immutable data is the highest-impact perf change you can describe in an interview.
code: @Component({ changeDetection: ChangeDetectionStrategy.OnPush })
deep:
What actually triggers change detection

zone.js monkey-patches async browser APIs — setTimeout, addEventListener, Promise, XHR/fetch. When any of them fires, Zone tells Angular “something might have changed,” and Angular runs change detection over the component tree, top to bottom.

Each pass re-evaluates every template binding and compares the new value to the previous one; if it differs, the DOM is updated.

Default vs OnPush

DefaultOnPush
When checkedevery component, every cycleonly when a trigger applies
Triggersany async event anywhere@Input reference change, event inside the component, async pipe emit, or manual markForCheck()
Costhighermuch lower in big trees

Why OnPush needs immutable data

OnPush compares @Input by reference. If you mutate an object/array in place, the reference is unchanged and the view won't update:

// ❌ mutation — same reference, OnPush won't see it
this.items.push(newItem);

// ✅ new reference — OnPush updates
this.items = [...this.items, newItem];

Forcing a check when you must

  • ChangeDetectorRef.markForCheck() — mark this component + ancestors to be checked next cycle (the OnPush-friendly one).
  • detectChanges() — run detection synchronously now, on this subtree.
  • detach() / reattach() — take a component out of the tree entirely for manual control.

Where Signals fit

Signals give Angular fine-grained reactivity — it knows exactly which template expressions depend on which signal, so it can update just those without walking the whole tree. This is the path to zoneless apps (drop zone.js entirely), which the framework is moving toward.

How to say it in an interview

“zone.js notifies Angular after async events and Default checks the whole tree. I switch hot components to OnPush, which only re-checks on input-reference changes, internal events, or async-pipe emissions — so I keep data immutable. Signals push this further toward zoneless, per-binding updates.”

question: List concrete performance optimisations in Angular.
answer:
• ChangeDetectionStrategy.OnPush + immutable data.
  • trackBy in *ngFor (or track in @for) to reuse DOM nodes.
  • Lazy load feature routes; add a preloading strategy for the rest.
  • Pure pipes instead of method calls in templates.
  • Virtual scrolling (cdk-virtual-scroll) for long lists.
  • async pipe to avoid manual-subscription leaks.
tip: Never call a method in a template binding — it runs on every change-detection cycle.

question: Pure vs impure pipes — and what is a pipe?
answer:
A pipe transforms a value in the template ({{ date | date:'short' }}).

Pure (default) — recomputes only when the input reference changes; cheap. Impure — runs every change-detection cycle (e.g. async, or a filter over a mutating array); powerful but costly, use sparingly.
tip: The async pipe is impure by necessity — it must react to new emissions.
code: @Pipe({ name: 'myFilter', pure: false })
deep:
What a pipe is

A pipe is a small class that transforms a value for display in a template, without changing the underlying data. You chain them with | and pass arguments after :.

{{ price | currency:'INR' }}
{{ date | date:'mediumDate' }}
{{ name | uppercase }}
{{ items | slice:0:5 }}

Pure vs impure

Pure (default)Impure
Runs wheninput reference changesevery change-detection cycle
Costcheapexpensive
Examplesdate, currency, uppercaseasync, a live filter/sort

The mutation trap

A pure pipe won't re-run if you mutate its input in place, because the reference didn't change:

this.items.push(x);       // ❌ pure filter pipe won't re-run
this.items = [...this.items, x]; // ✅ new reference, re-runs

Making the pipe impure (pure: false) fixes display but runs it on every cycle — a performance risk on large lists.

Don't filter/sort with pipes on big lists

The Angular team explicitly advises against filter/orderBy pipes (that's why they were removed from Angular). Prefer computing the filtered list in the component (or a computed() signal) so it only recalculates when inputs actually change.

@Pipe({ name: 'initials', standalone: true })
export class InitialsPipe implements PipeTransform {
  transform(name: string): string {
    return name.split(' ').map(w => w[0]).join('');
  }
}

How to say it in an interview

“A pipe transforms a value for display. Pure pipes — the default — only recompute when the input reference changes, so they’re cheap; impure pipes run every cycle. The async pipe is impure because it has to react to new emissions. I avoid filtering big lists in a pipe and do it in the component or a computed signal.”

question: What are Signals? How do they differ from RxJS?
answer:
Signals (v16+) are a fine-grained reactivity primitive: a wrapper around a value that notifies consumers when it changes, enabling precise, zone-less change detection.

vs RxJS: signals are synchronous, always hold a current value, and are ideal for local component state. RxJS is for streams / async events over time. They interoperate via toSignal / toObservable.
tip: Mention Signals even for older-Angular roles — it signals you keep up with the framework.
deep:
The three primitives

  • signal(v) — a writable box holding a value; read with count(), write with .set() / .update().
  • computed(fn) — a derived, read-only signal that recalculates only when a signal it reads changes (memoized).
  • effect(fn) — runs a side effect whenever any signal it reads changes (logging, syncing to storage).
count = signal(0);
double = computed(() => this.count() * 2);

this.count.set(5);      // double() is now 10
this.count.update(n => n + 1);

Signals vs RxJS

SignalObservable (RxJS)
Has a current valuealways (pull)no — values arrive over time (push)
Sync/asyncsynchronouscan be async
Best forlocal/UI stateevents, streams, HTTP, debounce
Operatorsfew (computed/effect)hundreds (map, switchMap…)
Subscriptionnone to managemust subscribe/unsubscribe

The mental model

A signal answers “what is the value right now?”. An observable answers “notify me each time a new value happens over time.” Use signals for state you render; use RxJS for asynchronous event pipelines.

They interoperate

// stream → signal (for templates)
user = toSignal(this.userService.user$, { initialValue: null });

// signal → stream (to use RxJS operators)
query$ = toObservable(this.query).pipe(debounceTime(300));

Why they matter

Signals enable fine-grained, zone-less change detection: Angular updates exactly the bindings that read a changed signal instead of re-checking the whole tree. That's the framework's direction going forward.

How to say it in an interview

“Signals are a synchronous reactivity primitive that always holds a current value — perfect for local component state and fine-grained change detection. RxJS is for asynchronous streams of values over time. They’re complementary, and toSignal/toObservable bridge them.”

question: What are Standalone Components and the new Angular Control Flow?
answer:
Standalone Components are Angular components that don't require an NgModule. Instead, they declare their own dependencies using the imports property, making applications simpler, easier to maintain, and better suited for lazy loading.

Angular's new built-in control flow (@if, @for, @switch, @let, and @defer) replaces structural directives like *ngIf and *ngFor with a faster, more readable syntax.
tip: Angular 17+ recommends Standalone APIs and the new Control Flow for all new applications.
deep:
What are Standalone Components?

Before Angular 14, every component had to belong to an NgModule. Standalone Components remove this requirement by allowing components, directives and pipes to manage their own dependencies.

@Component({
  standalone: true,
  imports: [CommonModule]
})
export class HomeComponent {}

Why were they introduced?

  • Reduce boilerplate.
  • Remove unnecessary NgModules.
  • Simplify dependency management.
  • Improve lazy loading.
  • Improve developer experience.
  • Better tree-shaking.

Bootstrapping a Standalone App

bootstrapApplication(AppComponent);

This replaces:

platformBrowserDynamic()
  .bootstrapModule(AppModule);

Standalone Imports

@Component({
  standalone: true,
  imports: [
    CommonModule,
    RouterModule,
    FormsModule,
    ChildComponent
  ]
})

Components import exactly what they need.

Standalone Directives and Pipes

Not only components, but directives and pipes can also be standalone.

@Directive({
  standalone: true
})

@Pipe({
  standalone: true
})

Traditional vs Standalone

NgModuleStandalone
Requires module declarations.No NgModule required.
More boilerplate.Cleaner code.
Harder lazy loading.Easier lazy loading.
Module-based.Component-based.

New Control Flow

Angular 17 introduced built-in control flow syntax.

OldNew
*ngIf@if
*ngFor@for
*ngSwitch@switch

@if Example

@if (loggedIn) {
  Welcome
} @else {
  Please Login
}

@for Example

@for (user of users; track user.id) {
  {{ user.name }}
} @empty {
  No users found.
}

The track expression is required and replaces trackBy.

@switch Example

@switch(status) {
  @case('loading') {
    Loading...
  }
  @case('success') {
    Success
  }
  @default {
    Error
  }
}

@let Example

@let total = items.length;

Total: {{ total }}

@let creates local template variables.

@defer Example

@defer {
  
} @loading {
  Loading...
}

@defer lazily renders expensive content to improve initial page load performance.

Why is the new Control Flow faster?

  • Built directly into Angular.
  • No structural directive instances.
  • Less generated code.
  • Better runtime performance.
  • Better type checking.
  • More readable templates.

Common Interview Questions

  • What is a Standalone Component? A component that doesn't require an NgModule.
  • Why were Standalone Components introduced? To reduce boilerplate and simplify Angular applications.
  • Can standalone components import other standalone components? Yes.
  • What replaces AppModule? bootstrapApplication().
  • What replaced *ngIf and *ngFor? @if and @for.
  • Why is track mandatory in @for? It helps Angular efficiently identify items during DOM updates.
  • What does @defer do? It delays rendering of expensive UI until needed.

How to say it in an interview

"Standalone Components remove the need for NgModules by allowing components to declare their own imports. They reduce boilerplate, improve lazy loading and simplify application structure. Angular 17 also introduced built-in control flow with @if, @for, @switch, @let and @defer, providing a cleaner syntax and better runtime performance than traditional structural directives."

question: What is View Encapsulation?
answer:
Controls how a component's styles are scoped:

  • Emulated (default) — Angular adds attribute selectors so styles don't leak; no real Shadow DOM.
  • ShadowDom — uses native Shadow DOM for true isolation.
  • None — styles are global.
tip: Use ::ng-deep sparingly to pierce encapsulation — it's deprecated and leaks globally.
code: @Component({ encapsulation: ViewEncapsulation.Emulated })
deep:
The problem it solves

Normally CSS is global — a .title rule in one component would style every .title on the page. View Encapsulation scopes a component's styles so they only apply to that component's own template.

The three modes

ModeHowIsolation
Emulated (default)Angular rewrites selectors and adds a unique attribute (_ngcontent-xyz) to each elementscoped, but no real DOM boundary
ShadowDomrenders the component in a native Shadow DOM treetrue, browser-enforced isolation
Nonestyles are injected globally, untouchednone — leaks everywhere

What Emulated actually generates

/* you write */
h1 { color: red; }

/* Angular emits */
h1[_ngcontent-abc] { color: red; }
<h1 _ngcontent-abc>...</h1>

So the rule can only match elements Angular tagged for this component — that's the "emulation" of scoping, done with plain attribute selectors (no Shadow DOM needed, works everywhere).

Piercing encapsulation

  • :host — style the component's own host element.
  • :host-context(.dark) — style based on an ancestor's class.
  • ::ng-deep — force a style into child components. Deprecated and effectively global — prefer restructuring or a shared style.

ShadowDom caveats

Real isolation also means global styles don't reach in, and events like focus retarget at the boundary. Great for true web-component reuse; overkill for a normal app.

How to say it in an interview

“By default Angular uses Emulated encapsulation — it adds a unique attribute to each element and rewrites my selectors so styles stay scoped without real Shadow DOM. ShadowDom gives true browser isolation, and None makes styles global. I avoid ::ng-deep since it’s deprecated and leaks.”

question: AOT vs JIT compilation.
answer:
AOT (Ahead-of-Time) compiles templates at build time — smaller bundles, faster rendering, template errors caught early, safer. It's the default for production.

JIT (Just-in-Time) compiles in the browser at runtime — historically used in development.
tip: Modern Angular (Ivy) uses AOT everywhere by default.
deep:
What is Angular Compilation?

Angular templates (.html) cannot be executed directly by the browser. Angular first compiles templates into efficient JavaScript instructions that create and update the DOM. This compilation can happen either before the application is deployed (AOT) or inside the browser at runtime (JIT).

1. JIT (Just-in-Time) Compilation

With JIT, Angular downloads both the application code and the Angular compiler. When the application starts, the browser compiles templates into executable JavaScript.

Developer writes:
Component + Template

Browser
    ↓
Angular Compiler
    ↓
Compiled JavaScript
    ↓
Render UI

Advantages:

  • Faster development builds.
  • Ideal during development because changes compile quickly.

Disadvantages:

  • Larger bundle because the Angular compiler is shipped to the browser.
  • Slower application startup.
  • Template errors are detected only when the application runs.

2. AOT (Ahead-of-Time) Compilation

With AOT, Angular compiles templates during the build process. The browser receives only optimized JavaScript and does not need the Angular compiler.

Developer writes:
Component + Template

Angular Build
    ↓
Angular Compiler
    ↓
Optimized JavaScript
    ↓
Deploy
    ↓
Browser renders immediately

Advantages:

  • Smaller bundle size.
  • Faster initial rendering.
  • Template errors are caught during build.
  • Improved security because templates are already compiled.

Disadvantages:

  • Slightly longer build time.

AOT vs JIT

FeatureAOTJIT
CompilationBuild TimeRuntime
Compiler in Bundle❌ No✅ Yes
Initial LoadFastSlower
Bundle SizeSmallerLarger
Template ErrorsBuild TimeRuntime
Used InProductionMainly Development (historically)

What is Ivy?

Ivy is Angular's rendering and compilation engine introduced in Angular 9. It replaced the older View Engine and significantly improved compilation speed, tree-shaking, debugging, and bundle size.

Unlike the old compiler, Ivy converts every component into highly optimized JavaScript instructions.

@Component({
  selector: 'app-user',
  template: '<h1>{{name}}</h1>'
})
export class UserComponent {}

Ivy internally generates optimized instructions similar to:

ɵɵelementStart(...)
ɵɵtext(...)
ɵɵelementEnd(...)

These low-level instructions are much smaller and faster than the metadata generated by the old View Engine.

How Ivy changed AOT

Before Angular 9, developers often used JIT during development and AOT for production. With Ivy, AOT became much faster, so modern Angular uses AOT by default for both development and production builds.

Ivy also enables:

  • Faster incremental builds.
  • Better tree shaking.
  • Smaller bundles.
  • Improved debugging.
  • Standalone Components.
  • Simpler library compilation.

Real-world Example

Imagine an enterprise Angular application with hundreds of components.

  • Using JIT, the browser downloads the Angular compiler and compiles every template when the application starts.
  • Using AOT + Ivy, all templates are already compiled during the build, so the browser immediately renders the application with a smaller bundle and faster startup.

Interview Answer

"AOT compiles Angular templates during the build, producing smaller bundles, faster startup, and compile-time template validation. JIT compiles templates in the browser at runtime and is mainly useful for development. Since Angular 9, Ivy became the default rendering engine, making AOT much faster and enabling Angular to use AOT by default while also improving tree shaking, build performance, and bundle size."

question: What does the static flag on @ViewChild do?
answer:
@ViewChild(ref, { static: true }) resolves the query before change detection, so it's available in ngOnInit — use only for elements that are always present.

static: false (default) resolves after the view initialises, available in ngAfterViewInit — required if the element is inside an *ngIf/*ngFor.
tip: If a ViewChild is undefined, it's almost always a static/timing issue.
code: @ViewChild('chart', { static: false }) chart!: ElementRef;

question: What are @HostListener and @HostBinding?
answer: @HostListener listens to events on the host element and executes a method when the event occurs. @HostBinding binds a property, attribute, class, or style of the host element to a class property. They are commonly used in custom directives to interact with the host element without directly manipulating the DOM.
tip: Use @HostListener for host events and @HostBinding to dynamically update the host element's classes, styles, or attributes.
deep:
What is @HostListener?

@HostListener listens for events fired by the host element and calls the decorated method.

@HostListener('click')
onClick() {
  console.log('Clicked');
}

You can also listen to global events.

@HostListener('window:resize')
onResize() {
  console.log(window.innerWidth);
}

What is @HostBinding?

@HostBinding binds a property of the host element to a class property.

@HostBinding('class.active')
isActive = true;

This automatically adds or removes the active CSS class.

Using Both Together

@Directive({
  selector: '[appToggle]'
})
export class ToggleDirective {

  @HostBinding('class.active')
  active = false;

  @HostListener('click')
  toggle() {
    this.active = !this.active;
  }
}

Common Uses

  • Highlight directives.
  • Hover effects.
  • Dropdown open/close.
  • Keyboard shortcuts.
  • Responsive behavior using window events.

Difference

@HostListener@HostBinding
Listens to host events.Binds host properties.
Triggers methods.Updates class, style or attribute.
Handles click, mouseenter, keydown, etc.Controls class, style, disabled, id, etc.

Common Interview Questions

  • What is @HostListener? It listens to events on the host element.
  • What is @HostBinding? It binds host element properties to class members.
  • Can they be used together? Yes, they are commonly used together in custom directives.
  • Can @HostListener listen to window events? Yes, for example window:resize or document:click.

How to say it in an interview

"@HostListener is used to listen to events on the host element, while @HostBinding binds properties, classes, styles or attributes of the host element. They are commonly used together in custom directives to add interactive behavior without directly manipulating the DOM."

question: Difference between ng-template, ng-container, and ng-content?
answer:
• ng-template defines a template that is not rendered until Angular explicitly creates it.
  • ng-container groups elements without creating an extra DOM element, making it useful with structural directives.
  • ng-content projects content provided by a parent component into a child component.
tip: ng-template stores content, ng-container groups content, and ng-content projects content.
code:
Welcome

 Loading...
deep:
ng-template

ng-template defines a chunk of template that Angular does not render immediately. It is rendered only when referenced by directives such as *ngIf, *ngTemplateOutlet, or other Angular APIs.


  Loading...




  Data Loaded

ng-container

ng-container groups elements without adding an extra element to the DOM.

It is useful when you need structural directives but don't want unnecessary wrapper elements.

ng-content

ng-content allows a parent component to project its HTML into a child component.



  
Profile

Difference

Featureng-templateng-containerng-content
Rendered immediately❌✅✅
Adds DOM element❌❌❌
Main purposeStore templateLogical groupingContent projection

When to use

  • ng-template → Conditional or reusable templates.
  • ng-container → Apply structural directives without extra DOM elements.
  • ng-content → Build reusable components that accept custom content.

Common Interview Questions

  • Does ng-container appear in the DOM? No.
  • When is ng-template rendered? Only when Angular creates it.
  • What is ng-content used for? Content projection from parent to child components.

How to say it in an interview

"ng-template stores template content that Angular renders only when needed. ng-container groups elements without creating an extra DOM element, making it useful with structural directives. ng-content is used for content projection, allowing a parent component to insert its own content into a child component."

question: Why is the async pipe preferred over manual subscription?
answer:
The async pipe subscribes to an Observable/Promise, renders the latest value, and automatically unsubscribes when the component is destroyed — eliminating a whole class of memory leaks.

It also plays well with OnPush: an emission marks the component for check.
tip: Use a single async pipe with *ngIf ... as to avoid multiple subscriptions to the same stream.
code: {{ user.name }}

question: Why use Renderer2 instead of direct DOM access?
answer:
Renderer2 abstracts DOM manipulation so your code works in environments without a real DOM (server-side rendering, web workers) and keeps Angular's security model intact.

Avoid document / nativeElement.innerHTML directly — they break SSR and open XSS risks.
tip: Mention SSR (Angular Universal) as the main reason to avoid direct DOM access.
code:
constructor(private r: Renderer2, private el: ElementRef) {}
ngOnInit() { this.r.addClass(this.el.nativeElement, 'active'); }
deep:
What is Renderer2?

Renderer2 is Angular's abstraction layer for manipulating the DOM. Instead of interacting directly with browser APIs like document or ElementRef.nativeElement, Angular provides Renderer2 so the framework can safely manage DOM updates across different rendering environments.

Why not manipulate the DOM directly?

Using browser APIs such as document.querySelector(), innerHTML, or style tightly couples your code to the browser. This causes problems when Angular runs in environments that don't have a real DOM, such as Server-Side Rendering (Angular Universal) or future rendering platforms.

// ❌ Avoid
const el = document.getElementById('title');
el.style.color = 'red';

// ❌ Avoid
this.elementRef.nativeElement.innerHTML = '<script>alert(1)</script>';

Direct DOM manipulation can also introduce Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerabilities if untrusted HTML is inserted into the page.

Using Renderer2

Renderer2 provides platform-independent methods for common DOM operations.

constructor(
  private renderer: Renderer2,
  private elementRef: ElementRef
) {}

ngOnInit() {
  this.renderer.addClass(this.elementRef.nativeElement, 'active');
}

Common Renderer2 Methods

MethodPurpose
createElement()Create a DOM element
appendChild()Append a child element
removeChild()Remove an element
setAttribute()Add or update attributes
removeAttribute()Remove attributes
setStyle()Apply CSS styles
removeStyle()Remove CSS styles
addClass()Add CSS class
removeClass()Remove CSS class
listen()Register event listeners
setProperty()Update DOM properties

Example: Dynamically Creating an Element

const button = this.renderer.createElement('button');
this.renderer.setProperty(button, 'textContent', 'Save');
this.renderer.addClass(button, 'btn-primary');
this.renderer.appendChild(this.elementRef.nativeElement, button);

Example: Listening to Events

this.renderer.listen(
  this.elementRef.nativeElement,
  'click',
  () => console.log('Clicked')
);

Renderer2 vs ElementRef

Renderer2ElementRef
Platform independentDirect browser DOM access
SaferCan bypass Angular protections
Works with SSRMay fail during SSR
Recommended for DOM changesUse only when absolutely necessary

Real-world Example

Suppose you're building a reusable Tooltip Directive.

  1. User hovers over an element.
  2. The directive dynamically creates a tooltip.
  3. The tooltip is appended to the DOM.
  4. Styles are applied.
  5. When the mouse leaves, the tooltip is removed.

All of these operations should use Renderer2 instead of direct DOM APIs.

const tooltip = this.renderer.createElement('div');
this.renderer.setProperty(tooltip, 'textContent', 'Customer Details');
this.renderer.addClass(tooltip, 'tooltip');
this.renderer.appendChild(document.body, tooltip);

What about Angular Bindings?

In most cases, you should not manipulate the DOM at all. Angular's template syntax already covers common scenarios.

  • Use [class.active] instead of addClass().
  • Use [style.color] instead of setStyle().
  • Use *ngIf instead of manually creating or removing elements.

Use Renderer2 only when you're writing directives or libraries that genuinely need low-level DOM manipulation.

Interview Tip

If asked 'Why Renderer2 instead of ElementRef?', don't answer only 'because it's safer.' Mention all three reasons: platform independence (SSR), security (avoids XSS), and Angular's rendering abstraction. Those are the key points interviewers expect.

Interview Answer

"Renderer2 is Angular's platform-independent API for DOM manipulation. Instead of accessing the DOM directly using document or ElementRef, Renderer2 works safely across browsers and Server-Side Rendering, integrates with Angular's rendering engine, and reduces security risks such as XSS. In practice, I use Angular bindings whenever possible, and only use Renderer2 when building directives or performing dynamic DOM manipulation."

question: What is an NgModule and what are its main metadata fields?
answer:
An @NgModule groups related code into a cohesive block. Key fields:

  • declarations — components, directives, pipes owned by this module.
  • imports — other modules whose exports this module needs.
  • exports — what this module makes available to importers.
  • providers — services (module-level DI).
  • bootstrap — the root component (AppModule only).
tip: Modern Angular is moving to standalone components — mention NgModules are no longer required in v17+.

question: What does ChangeDetectorRef do (markForCheck, detectChanges, detach)?
answer:
ChangeDetectorRef gives you manual control over change detection, mainly with OnPush:

  • markForCheck() — marks this component and ancestors to be checked in the next cycle (async data updated outside Angular).
  • detectChanges() — runs change detection on this component subtree immediately.
  • detach() / reattach() — remove/add the component from the CD tree for extreme perf tuning.
tip: With OnPush, if a value changes but the view doesn't update, you usually need markForCheck().
code:
constructor(private cdr: ChangeDetectorRef) {}
update() { this.data = next; this.cdr.markForCheck(); }

question: What is an InjectionToken and why use one?
answer: InjectionToken is a unique dependency injection token used for values that don't have a runtime class, such as interfaces, configuration objects, strings, numbers or feature flags. Since interfaces are removed during TypeScript compilation, Angular cannot use them as DI tokens. InjectionToken provides a type-safe way to inject these values.
tip: Use InjectionToken for application configuration, constants, and interface-based dependencies.
deep:
What is an InjectionToken?

InjectionToken creates a unique key that Angular's Dependency Injection system can use to resolve values that are not classes.

Why is it Needed?

Angular normally uses a class as the dependency injection token.

constructor(private userService: UserService) {}

However, interfaces, strings and primitive values do not exist at runtime.

interface ApiConfig {
  apiUrl: string;
}

Since interfaces disappear after TypeScript compilation, Angular cannot inject them directly.

Creating an InjectionToken

export const API_URL = new InjectionToken<string>('API_URL');

Providing a Value

providers: [
  {
    provide: API_URL,
    useValue: 'https://api.example.com'
  }
]

Injecting the Value

constructor(
  @Inject(API_URL)
  private apiUrl: string
) {}

Common Uses

  • Application configuration.
  • API URLs.
  • Feature flags.
  • Interface-based dependencies.
  • Shared constants.

Common Interview Questions

  • Why can't Angular inject an interface? Because interfaces are removed during TypeScript compilation.
  • When should you use InjectionToken? For values that don't have a runtime class.
  • Can InjectionToken be generic? Yes, for example InjectionToken<ApiConfig>.

How to say it in an interview

"Angular normally uses classes as dependency injection tokens. Since interfaces and primitive values don't exist at runtime, Angular cannot inject them directly. InjectionToken provides a unique, type-safe token that allows us to inject configuration objects, constants, feature flags and interface-based dependencies."

question: Explain useClass, useValue, useExisting, useFactory.
answer:
Angular providers tell the Dependency Injection (DI) system what object or value to provide when a dependency is requested.

  • useClass – Create an instance of the specified class. Useful for replacing one service implementation with another.
  • useValue – Provide a fixed value such as a configuration object, string, or number.
  • useExisting – Make one token use an existing service instance instead of creating a new one.
  • useFactory – Call a factory function to create the value. Useful when the value depends on runtime conditions or other services.

Use multi: true when multiple providers should be registered under the same token, such as HTTP interceptors.
tip: Remember it like this: useClass = new object, useValue = fixed value, useExisting = reuse existing object, useFactory = create dynamically.
deep:
What is a Provider?

A provider tells Angular's Dependency Injection system what should be returned when a dependency is requested.

useClass

Creates an instance of the specified class.

{ provide: Logger, useClass: ConsoleLogger }

Whenever Logger is injected, Angular creates a ConsoleLogger.

useValue

Returns a fixed value instead of creating an object.

{
  provide: API_URL,
  useValue: 'https://api.example.com'
}

Commonly used for configuration values and feature flags.

useExisting

Creates an alias to another provider. Both tokens share the same instance.

{
  provide: OldLogger,
  useExisting: Logger
}

No new object is created.

useFactory

Calls a function to create the dependency.

{
  provide: API_URL,
  useFactory: () => environment.apiUrl
}

The factory can also receive dependencies using the deps property.

{
  provide: Logger,
  useFactory: (config: AppConfig) => new Logger(config),
  deps: [APP_CONFIG]
}

Difference

ProviderReturnsCommon Use
useClassNew class instanceReplace service implementation
useValueFixed valueConfiguration and constants
useExistingExisting instanceCreate aliases
useFactoryFactory function resultRuntime or conditional creation

What is multi: true?

multi: true allows multiple providers to be registered for the same token.

{
  provide: HTTP_INTERCEPTORS,
  useClass: AuthInterceptor,
  multi: true
}

This is commonly used with HTTP interceptors.

Common Interview Questions

  • When do you use useValue? For configuration objects or constants.
  • What is the difference between useClass and useExisting? useClass creates a new instance, while useExisting reuses an existing one.
  • When is useFactory useful? When the dependency must be created dynamically or depends on other services.
  • Why use multi: true? To register multiple providers under a single token.

How to say it in an interview

"Angular providers tell the DI system what to return when a dependency is requested. useClass creates a class instance, useValue returns a fixed value, useExisting reuses an existing instance, and useFactory creates the dependency using a function. The multi: true option allows multiple providers for the same token, such as HTTP interceptors."

question: How do you create a custom pipe?
answer: Create a class that implements PipeTransform and decorate it with @Pipe. Implement the transform() method to receive the input value and optional arguments, then return the transformed result. Custom pipes should be pure and free of side effects.
tip: Use a pure pipe for display transformations. Avoid performing HTTP calls or modifying data inside a pipe.
deep:
Creating a Custom Pipe

A custom pipe is created by decorating a class with @Pipe and implementing the PipeTransform interface.

@Pipe({
  name: 'truncate',
  standalone: true
})
export class TruncatePipe implements PipeTransform {
  transform(value: string, max = 20): string {
    return value.length > max ? value.slice(0, max) + '...' : value;
  }
}

Using the Pipe

{{ description | truncate:30 }}

Pure vs Impure Pipes

Pure PipeImpure Pipe
Default behavior.Set pure: false.
Runs only when input reference changes.Runs during every change detection cycle.
Better performance.Can impact performance.

Best Practices

  • Keep pipes simple and reusable.
  • Avoid side effects.
  • Do not perform HTTP requests inside pipes.
  • Use pure pipes unless frequent updates are required.

Common Interview Questions

  • What interface does a custom pipe implement? PipeTransform.
  • Which method must be implemented? transform().
  • What is the default type of a pipe? Pure.
  • When would you use an impure pipe? Only when the displayed value changes without the input reference changing.

How to say it in an interview

"A custom pipe is created using the @Pipe decorator and the PipeTransform interface. The transformation logic is written in the transform() method. By default, pipes are pure, so they execute only when the input reference changes, making them efficient for formatting data in templates."

question: How do you build a custom attribute directive?
answer: Create a class decorated with @Directive and provide a selector. Attribute directives modify the appearance or behavior of an existing element. Use @HostListener to respond to host events, @HostBinding to update the host element, and @Input to accept values from the parent component.
tip: Prefer @HostListener and @HostBinding over directly accessing the DOM whenever possible.
deep:
What is an Attribute Directive?

An attribute directive changes the appearance or behavior of an existing DOM element without creating or removing elements.

Steps to Create One

  1. Create a class with the @Directive decorator.
  2. Provide a selector.
  3. Use @HostListener to handle events.
  4. Use @HostBinding to update the host element.
  5. Use @Input if the directive needs configuration.

Example with Input

@Input() appHighlight = 'yellow';

@HostListener('mouseenter')
onEnter() {
  this.background = this.appHighlight;
}

Usage:

Hello

Common Uses

  • Highlight elements.
  • Show tooltips.
  • Apply custom styles.
  • Restrict user input.
  • Add hover or focus effects.

Common Interview Questions

  • What is an attribute directive? A directive that changes the behavior or appearance of an existing element.
  • Which decorator creates a directive? @Directive.
  • How do you listen to host events? Using @HostListener.
  • How do you update the host element? Using @HostBinding.
  • Can a directive accept inputs? Yes, using @Input.

How to say it in an interview

"A custom attribute directive is created using the @Directive decorator. It modifies the behavior or appearance of an existing element without changing the DOM structure. Typically, @HostListener is used to respond to events, @HostBinding updates the host element, and @Input allows the directive to receive configuration from the parent."

question: How do custom structural directives work (TemplateRef + ViewContainerRef)?
answer: A structural directive changes the DOM by adding or removing elements. Angular passes the directive a TemplateRef, which represents the HTML template, and a ViewContainerRef, which represents the place where that template should be rendered. The directive can then decide whether to create or remove the view. This is how *ngIf works internally.
tip: Think of TemplateRef as what to render and ViewContainerRef as where to render it.
deep:
What is a Structural Directive?

A structural directive changes the structure of the DOM by adding or removing elements. Examples include *ngIf and *ngFor.

How Does it Work?

Angular automatically converts the * syntax into an <ng-template>.

<div *appIf="isLoggedIn">Welcome</div>

becomes:

<ng-template [appIf]="isLoggedIn">
  <div>Welcome</div>
</ng-template>

TemplateRef

TemplateRef represents the template that Angular can render.

Think of it as: What should be displayed?

ViewContainerRef

ViewContainerRef is the container where Angular inserts or removes the template.

Think of it as: Where should it be displayed?

Creating and Removing the View

this.viewContainer.clear();

if (show) {
  this.viewContainer.createEmbeddedView(this.template);
}
  • clear() removes any previously rendered view.
  • createEmbeddedView() inserts the template into the DOM.

How *ngIf Works Internally

  1. Angular converts *ngIf into an <ng-template>.
  2. The template is passed as a TemplateRef.
  3. The directive checks the condition.
  4. If true, it calls createEmbeddedView().
  5. If false, it removes the view using clear().

Common Interview Questions

  • What does TemplateRef represent? The template that can be rendered.
  • What does ViewContainerRef do? It inserts or removes embedded views from the DOM.
  • Why is there a * before structural directives? Angular rewrites it into an <ng-template>.
  • Which built-in directives use this mechanism? *ngIf and *ngFor.

How to say it in an interview

"A custom structural directive works by receiving a TemplateRef, which represents the template to render, and a ViewContainerRef, which represents where to render it. The directive decides whether to create or remove the embedded view. This is the same mechanism Angular uses internally for directives like *ngIf."

question: Input setter vs ngOnChanges — when to react to input changes?
answer: Both are used to react when an @Input value changes. An Input setter is ideal for handling or transforming a single input. ngOnChanges receives a SimpleChanges object containing all changed inputs, making it the better choice when logic depends on multiple inputs.
tip: Use an Input setter for a single input. Use ngOnChanges when you need to compare or react to multiple inputs together.
deep:
Input Setter

An Input setter is called whenever its associated @Input value changes. It is useful when only one input needs validation, transformation or additional processing.

@Input()
set id(value: number) {
  this.loadUser(value);
}

ngOnChanges

ngOnChanges is a lifecycle hook that runs whenever one or more @Input values change. It receives a SimpleChanges object containing the previous and current values.

ngOnChanges(changes: SimpleChanges) {
  if (changes['id']) {
    console.log(changes['id'].currentValue);
  }
}

Difference

Input SetterngOnChanges
Handles one input.Handles multiple inputs.
Simple and concise.Provides previous and current values.
Good for validation or transformation.Best when logic depends on several inputs.

Execution Order

During the first change detection cycle, ngOnChanges executes before ngOnInit.

Common Interview Questions

  • When should you use an Input setter? When reacting to a single input.
  • When should you use ngOnChanges? When multiple inputs need to be processed together.
  • What does SimpleChanges contain? Previous value, current value and first change information.
  • Which lifecycle hook runs before ngOnInit? ngOnChanges.

How to say it in an interview

"I use an Input setter when I need to react to a single input because it's simple and keeps the logic close to that property. If my logic depends on multiple inputs or I need access to previous and current values, I use ngOnChanges, which provides all changed inputs through the SimpleChanges object."

question: How do you make a custom two-way bindable property?
answer: A custom two-way binding is created using an @Input() property and an @Output() event named <property>Change. Angular's [(property)] syntax is simply a shortcut for combining property binding and event binding. In Angular 17.2+, the model() API provides a simpler way to create two-way bindable properties.
tip: Remember: value + valueChange enables [(value)].
deep:
What is Two-Way Binding?

Two-way binding keeps data synchronized between a parent component and a child component.

Angular's [(value)] syntax is called banana-in-a-box syntax.

How Does it Work?

This syntax is just shorthand for combining property binding and event binding.

<app-counter [(value)]="count"></app-counter>

is equivalent to:

<app-counter
  [value]="count"
  (valueChange)="count = $event">
</app-counter>

Child Component

@Input() value = 0;
@Output() valueChange = new EventEmitter<number>();

update(newValue: number) {
  this.value = newValue;
  this.valueChange.emit(newValue);
}

The important rule is that the output event must be named <property>Change.

Angular 17.2+ (model)

Modern Angular also supports the model() API, which automatically creates a two-way bindable property.

value = model(0);

This replaces the need to manually create both @Input() and @Output() for many use cases.

Common Interview Questions

  • How does [(value)] work internally? It combines [value] and (valueChange).
  • Why must the event be named valueChange? Angular follows the <property>Change naming convention to enable two-way binding.
  • Can you create custom two-way binding? Yes, by using @Input() and @Output(), or model() in modern Angular.

How to say it in an interview

"To create custom two-way binding, define an @Input() property and an @Output() event named <property>Change. Angular's [(property)] syntax is shorthand for [property] plus (propertyChange). In Angular 17.2 and later, the model() API provides a simpler way to achieve the same behavior."

question: What is a FormArray and when do you use it?
answer: FormArray is a collection of form controls or form groups stored as an indexed array. It is used when the number of form fields is dynamic and can change at runtime, such as multiple phone numbers, addresses, or order items. You can add or remove controls using methods like push() and removeAt().
tip: Remember: FormControl = one field, FormGroup = fixed set of fields, FormArray = dynamic list of fields.
deep:
What is FormArray?

FormArray represents a dynamic list of form controls or form groups. Unlike FormGroup, the number of elements is not fixed and can grow or shrink at runtime.

When Should You Use It?

  • Multiple phone numbers.
  • Multiple email addresses.
  • Order items in a shopping cart.
  • Dynamic survey questions.
  • Any form where users can add or remove fields.

Creating a FormArray

phones = this.fb.array([
  this.fb.control('')
]);

Adding and Removing Controls

addPhone() {
  this.phones.push(this.fb.control(''));
}

removePhone(index: number) {
  this.phones.removeAt(index);
}

Displaying in the Template

<div formArrayName="phones">
  <div *ngFor="let phone of phones.controls; let i = index">
    <input [formControlName]="i">
  </div>
</div>

FormControl vs FormGroup vs FormArray

TypeRepresentsUse Case
FormControlSingle input fieldName, Email
FormGroupFixed collection of controlsRegistration Form
FormArrayDynamic list of controls/groupsPhone numbers, Addresses, Order items

Common Interview Questions

  • When should you use FormArray? When the number of fields is dynamic.
  • Can a FormArray contain FormGroups? Yes. A FormArray can contain FormControls, FormGroups, or even other FormArrays.
  • How do you add a new control? Using push().
  • How do you remove a control? Using removeAt(index).

How to say it in an interview

"A FormArray is used when the number of form fields is dynamic. It stores controls or groups in an indexed array, allowing controls to be added or removed at runtime. Typical examples include phone numbers, addresses, or order items. Unlike FormGroup, which has a fixed structure, FormArray is designed for variable-length forms."

question: How do you write sync and async custom validators?
answer: A synchronous (sync) validator immediately checks a form control and returns either ValidationErrors or null. An asynchronous (async) validator performs validation that takes time, such as calling a server, and returns an Observable or Promise that eventually emits ValidationErrors or null. Sync validators are used for local validation, while async validators are commonly used to check values like whether a username or email already exists.
tip: Use sync validators for local checks and async validators for server-side validation. Combine async validators with updateOn: 'blur' to avoid unnecessary API calls.
deep:
What is a Custom Validator?

A custom validator is a function that checks whether a form control's value is valid according to your own business rules.

Synchronous Validator

A synchronous validator runs immediately whenever validation is triggered.

function noSpaces(control: AbstractControl): ValidationErrors | null {
  return /\s/.test(control.value)
    ? { spaces: true }
    : null;
}

If the value is valid, return null. Otherwise, return a validation error object.

Asynchronous Validator

An asynchronous validator is used when validation depends on an external source, such as a backend API.

function usernameTaken(control: AbstractControl) {
  return userService.checkUsername(control.value).pipe(
    map(exists => exists ? { usernameTaken: true } : null)
  );
}

Async validators return an Observable or Promise.

Adding Validators

new FormControl('', {
  validators: [Validators.required, noSpaces],
  asyncValidators: [usernameTaken],
  updateOn: 'blur'
});

Sync vs Async Validators

Sync ValidatorAsync Validator
Runs immediately.Runs asynchronously.
Returns ValidationErrors | null.Returns Observable<ValidationErrors | null> or Promise<ValidationErrors | null>.
Used for local validation.Used for server-side validation.
No network request.Often calls an API.

Common Interview Questions

  • When should you use an async validator? When validation requires a server call, such as checking whether a username or email already exists.
  • Why does an async validator return an Observable or Promise? Because the result is not available immediately.
  • What should a validator return when the value is valid? null.
  • Why use updateOn: 'blur' with async validators? To avoid making an API call on every keystroke.

How to say it in an interview

"A synchronous validator performs immediate validation and returns ValidationErrors or null. An asynchronous validator is used when validation depends on external resources, such as checking a username against a database. It returns an Observable or Promise that eventually emits the validation result. Angular allows both to be configured on the same form control."

question: How do you read route params — snapshot vs observable?
answer: Inject ActivatedRoute to access route parameters. Use snapshot.paramMap when the parameter is read only once during component initialization. Use the paramMap observable when the same component can remain active while route parameters change, allowing the component to react automatically.
tip: Use snapshot for one-time reads and paramMap when route parameters can change without recreating the component.
deep:
Using snapshot

snapshot provides the current route parameters synchronously.

const id = this.route.snapshot.paramMap.get('id');

Use it when the component is recreated for each navigation.

Using paramMap Observable

paramMap emits whenever route parameters change.

this.route.paramMap.subscribe(params => {
  const id = params.get('id');
  this.load(id);
});

This is useful when navigating between routes like /users/1 → /users/2 while Angular reuses the same component.

Difference

snapshotparamMap
Reads parameters once.Listens for parameter changes.
Synchronous.Observable.
Doesn't update automatically.Updates whenever parameters change.

Query Parameters

For query parameters, use queryParamMap or snapshot.queryParamMap.

const page = this.route.snapshot.queryParamMap.get('page');

Common Interview Questions

  • When should you use snapshot? When the parameter is read only once.
  • When should you use paramMap? When the component should react to parameter changes.
  • What is the difference between route params and query params? Route params are part of the URL path, while query params appear after ?.

How to say it in an interview

"I use snapshot.paramMap when I only need the route parameter once during initialization. If the same component can be reused with different route parameters, I subscribe to paramMap so the component automatically responds to parameter changes."

question: What does a modern functional route guard look like?
answer: Modern Angular applications use CanActivateFn to create functional route guards. Instead of a class, a guard is simply a function that uses inject() to access services. A guard can return true, false, a UrlTree, an Observable, or a Promise.
tip: Return a UrlTree instead of calling router.navigate() inside the guard.
deep:
Functional Route Guards

Since Angular 14, route guards can be written as functions instead of classes. They are simpler, require less boilerplate and use the inject() function to access dependencies.

export const authGuard: CanActivateFn = () => {
  const auth = inject(AuthService);
  return auth.isLoggedIn();
};

Possible Return Values

Return TypeMeaning
trueAllow navigation.
falseBlock navigation.
UrlTreeRedirect to another route.
Observable<boolean | UrlTree>Async permission check.
Promise<boolean | UrlTree>Async permission check.

Why Return UrlTree?

Returning a UrlTree lets the Angular Router handle the redirect as part of the navigation process. It is cleaner than calling router.navigate() inside the guard.

Common Uses

  • Authentication.
  • Role-based authorization.
  • Feature access control.
  • Preventing unauthorized navigation.

Common Interview Questions

  • What is a functional guard? A route guard written as a function using CanActivateFn.
  • How do you access services? Using inject().
  • What can a guard return? boolean, UrlTree, Observable, or Promise.
  • Why return a UrlTree? It performs redirects through the router instead of manually navigating.

How to say it in an interview

"Modern Angular uses functional guards with CanActivateFn. They use inject() instead of constructor injection and can return a boolean, UrlTree, Observable or Promise. Returning a UrlTree is the recommended way to redirect users during navigation."

question: How do you handle HttpClient errors and retries?
answer: Pipe the request through retry(n) for transient failures and catchError to map/handle the error (log, show a message, return a fallback). Centralise cross-cutting handling in an HTTP interceptor.
tip: retry then catchError is the standard resilience pattern for flaky endpoints.
code:
this.http.get<User>(url).pipe(
  retry(2),
  catchError(err => { this.notify(err); return of(null); })
)

question: What are route preloading strategies?
answer: Lazy routes load on demand. A preloading strategy fetches some lazy bundles in the background after the app boots, so navigation feels instant. Built-in PreloadAllModules, or a custom strategy that preloads only routes flagged in their data.
tip: PreloadAllModules gives lazy-loading's small initial bundle plus fast later navigation.
code: provideRouter(routes, withPreloading(PreloadAllModules))

question: ViewChild vs ViewChildren (and QueryList).
