answer: @ViewChild retrieves the first matching element, directive, or component from the view. @ViewChildren returns a QueryList containing all matching elements. QueryList automatically updates when the view changes, and you can subscribe to its changes observable to react to items being added or removed.
tip: Access @ViewChild and @ViewChildren in ngAfterViewInit(). Use QueryList.changes when child components are added or removed dynamically.
deep:
@ViewChild

@ViewChild returns the first matching element, directive or component from the component's own template.

@ViewChild('searchInput')
input!: ElementRef;

@ViewChildren

@ViewChildren returns a QueryList containing all matching elements.

@ViewChildren(ItemComponent)
items!: QueryList<ItemComponent>;

What is QueryList?

QueryList is an Angular collection that automatically updates when matching child elements are added or removed from the view.

this.items.changes.subscribe(() => {
  console.log(this.items.length);
});

Difference

@ViewChild@ViewChildren
Returns one match.Returns all matches.
Single reference.Returns a QueryList.
Used for one child.Used for multiple children.

Lifecycle

View queries are available after ngAfterViewInit() because the component's view has been fully initialized.

@ContentChildren

@ContentChildren works similarly to @ViewChildren, but queries projected content provided through <ng-content>.

Common Interview Questions

  • What is the difference between ViewChild and ViewChildren? ViewChild returns one match, while ViewChildren returns a QueryList of all matches.
  • What is QueryList? An Angular collection that automatically updates as the view changes.
  • Which lifecycle hook should be used? ngAfterViewInit.
  • How do you detect dynamically added child components? Subscribe to QueryList.changes.

How to say it in an interview

"I use @ViewChild when I need a single child component or element, and @ViewChildren when I need all matching children. @ViewChildren returns a QueryList, which automatically updates as the view changes, and I can subscribe to its changes observable to react to dynamically added or removed children."

question: How do you load a component dynamically?
answer: Normally, components are declared in the template. Dynamic component loading allows Angular to create a component at runtime instead. This is useful when the component to display is not known until the application is running, such as dialogs, dashboards, or plugin systems. In modern Angular, use ViewContainerRef.createComponent() to create the component. The older ComponentFactoryResolver approach is deprecated.
tip: Use dynamic components when the component is chosen at runtime, not when it's known at development time.
deep:
What is Dynamic Component Loading?

Dynamic component loading means creating a component while the application is running instead of declaring it directly in the HTML template.

Normal vs Dynamic Components

Static component:

<app-widget></app-widget>

The component is known when the application is built.

Dynamic component:

this.container.createComponent(MyWidgetComponent);

The component is chosen and created at runtime.

Common Use Cases

  • Dialogs and modal windows.
  • Widget dashboards.
  • Plugin-based applications.
  • Dynamic tabs.
  • Components selected based on user actions or API responses.

Example

@ViewChild('container', { read: ViewContainerRef })
container!: ViewContainerRef;

const ref = this.container.createComponent(MyWidgetComponent);
ref.setInput('title', 'Hello');

createComponent() creates the component and inserts it into the ViewContainerRef.

Passing Data

After creating the component, inputs can be set using:

ref.setInput('title', 'Hello');

Removing the Component

ref.destroy();

or

this.container.clear();

Common Interview Questions

  • When should you load a component dynamically? When the component is determined at runtime.
  • Which API is used in modern Angular? ViewContainerRef.createComponent().
  • What replaced ComponentFactoryResolver? createComponent().
  • How do you pass data to a dynamically created component? Using componentRef.setInput().

How to say it in an interview

"Dynamic component loading is used when the component to display is not known until runtime. Modern Angular creates components using ViewContainerRef.createComponent(). After creation, inputs can be passed with setInput(), and the component can be removed using destroy() or by clearing the ViewContainerRef. This is commonly used for dialogs, dashboards, dynamic tabs, and plugin-based applications."

question: How does Angular's animation system work?
answer: Angular animations are created using @angular/animations. You define animation triggers with states and transitions, then bind them in the template using [@triggerName]. Special transitions like :enter and :leave are commonly used for elements being added or removed from the DOM.
tip: Use CSS transitions or animations for simple UI effects. Use Angular animations for state-based, route, or complex animations.
deep:
How Angular Animations Work

Angular animations are defined using animation triggers. A trigger contains one or more states and transitions that describe how an element should animate.

animations: [
  trigger('fade', [
    transition(':enter', [
      style({ opacity: 0 }),
      animate('200ms', style({ opacity: 1 }))
    ])
  ])
]

Apply the animation in the template.

Common Animation Functions

  • trigger() – Defines an animation.
  • state() – Defines a named animation state.
  • transition() – Defines when an animation runs.
  • style() – Sets CSS styles.
  • animate() – Specifies duration and easing.

:enter and :leave

:enter runs when an element is added to the DOM, while :leave runs before an element is removed.

When to Use Angular Animations

  • Route transitions.
  • Dialogs and overlays.
  • State-based UI animations.
  • Complex animation sequences.

When CSS is Better

  • Hover effects.
  • Button animations.
  • Simple fades.
  • Simple transitions.

Common Interview Questions

  • How do you apply an Angular animation? Define a trigger and bind it using [@triggerName].
  • What are :enter and :leave? Built-in transitions for elements entering and leaving the DOM.
  • When would you use CSS instead of Angular animations? For simple hover, fade or transition effects where Angular's animation engine isn't needed.

How to say it in an interview

"Angular animations are built using triggers, states and transitions from @angular/animations. The trigger is attached to an element using [@triggerName]. For simple UI effects I usually prefer CSS transitions, while Angular animations are better suited for route transitions, dialogs and more complex state-based animations."

question: What is SSR (Server-Side Rendering) and Hydration in Angular?
answer: Server-Side Rendering (SSR) renders an Angular application into HTML on the server before sending it to the browser. This improves SEO, reduces the time to display content, and provides a faster initial page load. Hydration is the process where the browser reuses the HTML generated by the server and attaches Angular's event listeners instead of rebuilding the entire page. This avoids unnecessary DOM recreation and prevents UI flickering.
tip: Think of SSR as creating the page on the server and Hydration as making that page interactive in the browser.
deep:
What is SSR?

Server-Side Rendering (SSR) means Angular generates the HTML on the server instead of waiting for JavaScript to run in the browser.

Without SSR

  1. Browser downloads HTML.
  2. Downloads Angular JavaScript.
  3. Angular starts.
  4. Page is rendered.

With SSR

  1. Request reaches the server.
  2. Angular renders the page on the server.
  3. Ready-to-display HTML is sent to the browser.
  4. The user immediately sees the content.

Benefits of SSR

  • Better SEO because search engines can easily read the HTML.
  • Faster First Contentful Paint (FCP).
  • Better user experience on slow networks or devices.

What is Hydration?

After the server sends the HTML, Angular starts in the browser and attaches itself to the existing HTML instead of recreating it.

This process is called Hydration.

Without hydration, Angular would destroy the server-rendered DOM and render it again, which could cause a visible flicker.

Enable Hydration

bootstrapApplication(AppComponent, {
  providers: [
    provideClientHydration()
  ]
});

Things to Remember

  • Avoid directly using window, document, or localStorage because they don't exist on the server.
  • Use isPlatformBrowser() when browser-only code is required.
  • Prefer Angular APIs such as Renderer2 for DOM manipulation.

SSR vs CSR

SSRCSR (Default Angular)
HTML generated on the server.HTML generated in the browser.
Better SEO.SEO is less effective without additional configuration.
Faster initial page display.User waits for JavaScript before seeing content.

Common Interview Questions

  • Why use SSR? To improve SEO and the initial page load.
  • What is hydration? Reusing the server-rendered HTML and attaching Angular to it instead of rendering again.
  • Why shouldn't you directly use window in SSR? Because browser APIs don't exist on the server.
  • How do you check if code is running in the browser? Using isPlatformBrowser().

How to say it in an interview

"SSR renders the Angular application on the server and sends ready-to-display HTML to the browser. This improves SEO and makes the initial page load faster. Hydration then makes that HTML interactive by attaching Angular to the existing DOM instead of recreating it, which improves performance and avoids UI flickering."

question: What are deferrable views (@defer)?
answer: @defer (introduced in Angular 17) delays loading and rendering part of a template until it is needed. Instead of downloading everything during the initial page load, Angular loads deferred content later based on triggers such as on viewport, on idle, on interaction, or on hover. This reduces the initial bundle size and improves application performance.
tip: Use @defer for heavy components like charts, maps, editors, or dashboards that don't need to load immediately.
deep:
What is @defer?

@defer allows Angular to delay loading a part of the template until it is actually needed.

This improves performance because users don't have to download code for components they may never see.

Why Do We Need It?

Imagine a dashboard that contains a large chart at the bottom of the page.

  • Without @defer, the chart is downloaded immediately.
  • With @defer, Angular waits until the user reaches the chart before downloading it.

This makes the application load faster.

Common Triggers

TriggerWhen it loads
on viewportWhen the content enters the viewport.
on idleWhen the browser becomes idle.
on interactionAfter the user clicks or interacts.
on hoverWhen the user hovers over an element.
whenWhen a specified condition becomes true.

Example

@defer (on viewport) {
  <heavy-chart />
} @placeholder {
  <div>Loading chart...</div>
} @loading {
  <div>Please wait...</div>
} @error {
  <div>Failed to load chart.</div>
}

Supporting Blocks

  • @placeholder – Displayed before loading starts.
  • @loading – Displayed while the deferred content is loading.
  • @error – Displayed if loading fails.

Common Use Cases

  • Charts and graphs.
  • Maps.
  • Rich text editors.
  • Large tables.
  • Analytics dashboards.
  • Widgets below the fold.

Benefits

  • Smaller initial JavaScript bundle.
  • Faster page load.
  • Better Core Web Vitals.
  • Improved user experience.

Common Interview Questions

  • What problem does @defer solve? It delays loading code that isn't immediately needed, improving performance.
  • Name some defer triggers. on viewport, on idle, on interaction, on hover, and when.
  • What is the difference between @placeholder and @loading? @placeholder is shown before loading starts, while @loading is shown during the loading process.
  • When should you use @defer? For heavy UI that isn't required immediately.

How to say it in an interview

"@defer is an Angular 17 feature that lazily loads part of a template only when it's needed. It supports triggers such as on viewport, on idle, and on interaction, helping reduce the initial bundle size and improve application performance. Angular also provides @placeholder, @loading, and @error blocks to improve the user experience during loading."

question: What are signal inputs (input()) in Angular?
answer: input() is the signal-based replacement for @Input(). It creates a read-only signal that automatically updates when the parent passes a new value. Because it is a signal, it works naturally with computed() and effect(), reducing the need for ngOnChanges.
tip: Think of input() as @Input() + Signals.
deep:
What is input()?

input() is the modern signal-based way to receive data from a parent component.

Instead of:

@Input() name!: string;

you can write:

name = input.required<string>();

Benefits

  • Works directly with Signals.
  • No need for ngOnChanges in many cases.
  • Easy to derive values using computed().
  • Strong typing with input.required().

Common Interview Questions

  • What replaces @Input() in modern Angular? input().
  • Is input() writable? No. It is a read-only signal updated by the parent.

How to say it in an interview

"Signal inputs are the modern alternative to @Input(). They expose input values as signals, allowing them to work naturally with computed() and effect(), reducing the need for lifecycle hooks like ngOnChanges."

question: What are signal queries in Angular?
answer: Signal queries are the signal-based versions of @ViewChild, @ViewChildren, @ContentChild, and @ContentChildren. Instead of decorators, they return signals whose values automatically update when the queried element or component changes.
tip: Think of viewChild() as the signal version of @ViewChild.

question: How does Angular protect against XSS?
answer: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) is an attack where malicious JavaScript is injected into a web page. Angular helps prevent XSS by treating all bound values as untrusted and automatically sanitizing them based on the binding context (HTML, URL, Style, etc.). Interpolation ({{ }}) automatically escapes HTML, and bindings such as [innerHTML] are sanitized before being rendered. Only use DomSanitizer.bypassSecurityTrust... when you completely trust the content.
tip: Angular is secure by default. Only bypass sanitization for content from trusted sources.
code: // Angular sanitizes the HTML before rendering
deep:
What is XSS?

Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) is a security attack where an attacker injects malicious JavaScript into a web page.

For example, instead of harmless HTML, a user might submit:

<script>alert('Hacked')</script>

If the browser executes this script, it can steal cookies, access user data, or perform unwanted actions.

How Angular Prevents XSS

Angular assumes that all values coming from users or external sources are untrusted.

  • Interpolation ({{ }}) escapes HTML automatically.
  • Bindings such as [innerHTML] are sanitized before rendering.
  • URLs and styles are also sanitized when necessary.

Example

<div>{{ userHtml }}</div>

The HTML is displayed as plain text, not executed.

<div [innerHTML]="userHtml"></div>

Angular removes unsafe HTML before rendering it.

DomSanitizer

Sometimes you need to display trusted HTML, such as content from your own CMS.

safeHtml = sanitizer.bypassSecurityTrustHtml(html);

This tells Angular not to sanitize the value.

Use this only for content you fully trust. Using it with user-provided content can introduce XSS vulnerabilities.

Common Interview Questions

  • What is XSS? A security attack that injects malicious JavaScript into a web page.
  • How does Angular prevent XSS? By automatically escaping or sanitizing untrusted values.
  • Is interpolation safe? Yes. Angular escapes HTML automatically.
  • When should you use DomSanitizer? Only when rendering trusted HTML or other trusted resources.
  • Why is bypassSecurityTrustHtml() dangerous? It disables Angular's built-in protection, so untrusted content could execute malicious code.

How to say it in an interview

"Angular protects against XSS by treating all binding values as untrusted and automatically sanitizing them based on their context. Interpolation escapes HTML, while bindings like [innerHTML] are sanitized before rendering. If trusted content must bypass sanitization, Angular provides DomSanitizer, but it should be used carefully because it disables Angular's built-in XSS protection."

question: Smart (container) vs dumb (presentational) components.
answer: Smart/container components fetch data, hold state, and talk to services/the store. Dumb/presentational components just take @Inputs and emit @Outputs — no dependencies, easy to test and reuse. This separation keeps UI reusable and logic centralised.
tip: Presentational components pair perfectly with OnPush since they're pure input → view.

question: What is ControlValueAccessor and when do you implement it?
answer:
ControlValueAccessor (CVA) is an Angular interface that allows a custom component to behave like a built-in form control such as an input or select. After implementing it, the component works with [(ngModel)], formControl, formControlName, validation, and form APIs.

It requires four methods: writeValue() updates the view from the model, registerOnChange() notifies Angular when the value changes, registerOnTouched() marks the control as touched, and setDisabledState() updates the disabled state.
tip: Implement ControlValueAccessor whenever you build a reusable custom form component like a rating, date picker, color picker, or toggle.
deep:
What is ControlValueAccessor?

ControlValueAccessor is the bridge between Angular Forms and a custom component. It allows Angular to treat your component exactly like a native <input>, <select>, or <textarea>.

Why Do We Need It?

Imagine you create a custom rating component:

<app-rating></app-rating>

Angular doesn't know how to read or write its value automatically. By implementing ControlValueAccessor, your component can participate in Angular Forms just like a built-in form control.

The Four Methods

MethodPurpose
writeValue(value)Updates the component when Angular changes the model.
registerOnChange(fn)Saves a callback to notify Angular when the user changes the value.
registerOnTouched(fn)Saves a callback to mark the control as touched.
setDisabledState(isDisabled)Updates the component when Angular enables or disables the control.

Registering the Control

providers: [{
  provide: NG_VALUE_ACCESSOR,
  useExisting: RatingComponent,
  multi: true
}]

The multi: true option registers your component as one of Angular's value accessors.

When Should You Use It?

  • Custom rating component.
  • Date picker.
  • Color picker.
  • Rich text editor.
  • Toggle or switch.
  • Any reusable custom form control.

Common Interview Questions

  • Why do we implement ControlValueAccessor? To make a custom component work with Angular Forms.
  • How many methods does it require? Four.
  • What does writeValue() do? Updates the view from the model.
  • What does registerOnChange() do? Lets the component notify Angular when the value changes.
  • Why is NG_VALUE_ACCESSOR required? It tells Angular to use the component as a form value accessor.

How to say it in an interview

"ControlValueAccessor is an interface that lets a custom component behave like a native Angular form control. After implementing it, the component works with Reactive Forms, Template-driven Forms, validation, and two-way binding. The four required methods handle writing values to the view, notifying Angular of value changes, marking the control as touched, and updating its disabled state."

question: How do you validate across multiple fields (e.g. password match)?
answer: Cross-field validation is used when a validation rule depends on more than one form field, such as checking whether the password and confirm password match. Since a single FormControl can only access its own value, the validator should be added to the FormGroup, where it can access all sibling controls.
tip: If a validation rule compares two or more fields, place the validator on the FormGroup, not on an individual control.
deep:
What is Cross-Field Validation?

Cross-field validation is used when a validation rule depends on the values of multiple form controls.

Example

  • Password and Confirm Password must match.
  • Start Date must be before End Date.
  • Minimum Amount must be less than Maximum Amount.

Why Use a FormGroup Validator?

A FormControl only knows about its own value. To compare multiple fields, Angular needs access to the parent FormGroup, which contains all the controls.

Example

function matchPasswords(group: AbstractControl): ValidationErrors | null {
  const password = group.get('password')?.value;
  const confirm = group.get('confirmPassword')?.value;

  return password === confirm
    ? null
    : { passwordMismatch: true };
}

Register the validator on the FormGroup:

this.fb.group(
  {
    password: [''],
    confirmPassword: ['']
  },
  {
    validators: matchPasswords
  }
);

Where is the Error Stored?

By default, the validation error is stored on the FormGroup.

form.errors
// { passwordMismatch: true }

If needed, you can also set an error on a specific control using setErrors(), though returning the error from the group validator is the common approach.

Common Interview Questions

  • Why can't a FormControl validate password confirmation? Because it only has access to its own value.
  • Where should a cross-field validator be added? On the FormGroup.
  • Where is the validation error stored? On the FormGroup unless you explicitly assign it to a control.
  • Give examples of cross-field validation. Password confirmation, date range validation, and minimum/maximum value checks.

How to say it in an interview

"Cross-field validation is implemented by adding a validator to the FormGroup because it needs access to multiple controls. The validator compares sibling values, such as password and confirm password, and returns a validation error if the rule fails. This approach is used whenever validation depends on more than one field."

question: What are typed reactive forms (v14+)?
answer: Since Angular 14, reactive forms are strongly typed. FormControl, FormGroup, and FormArray preserve the value types, providing better type safety, autocomplete, and compile-time error checking. Use nonNullable: true to prevent a control's value from being null.
tip: Use nonNullable: true when a form control should never contain null.
deep:
What are Typed Reactive Forms?

Typed Reactive Forms, introduced in Angular 14, provide compile-time type checking for reactive forms. This reduces runtime errors and improves IDE autocomplete.

email = new FormControl('', {
  nonNullable: true
});

Here, email.value is inferred as string.

Without nonNullable

email = new FormControl('');
// email.value: string | null

With nonNullable

email = new FormControl('', {
  nonNullable: true
});
// email.value: string

Benefits

  • Compile-time type safety.
  • Better IDE autocomplete.
  • Fewer runtime errors.
  • Easier refactoring.

Difference

Before Angular 14Angular 14+
Weakly typed forms.Strongly typed forms.
More casting required.Better type inference.
Less compile-time checking.Compile-time validation.

Common Interview Questions

  • What are typed forms? Reactive forms with compile-time type safety.
  • What is the benefit of nonNullable? It removes null from the control's value type.
  • When were typed forms introduced? Angular 14.

How to say it in an interview

"Typed Reactive Forms were introduced in Angular 14 to provide compile-time type safety. They improve autocomplete, catch type errors during development, and reduce the need for casting. I typically use nonNullable: true for controls that should never contain null values."

question: How do you react to form changes reactively?
answer: Every control/group exposes valueChanges and statusChanges as Observables. Pipe them (e.g. debounceTime + switchMap) for autosave, dependent fields, or live search. Set updateOn: 'blur' to reduce noise.
tip: valueChanges + debounceTime is the clean autosave / dependent-field pattern.

question: What does updateOn: 'blur' | 'submit' do?
answer: The updateOn option controls when a form control updates its value and runs validation. By default ('change'), updates happen on every keystroke. With 'blur', updates occur when the input loses focus. With 'submit', updates and validation run only when the form is submitted.
tip: Use updateOn: 'blur' for expensive or async validation to avoid validating on every keystroke.
deep:
What is updateOn?

updateOn determines when Angular updates a form control's value and performs validation.

Available Options

OptionWhen it updates
changeOn every value change (default).
blurWhen the input loses focus.
submitWhen the form is submitted.

Example

const email = new FormControl('', {
  validators: [Validators.required],
  updateOn: 'blur'
});

Here, validation runs only after the user leaves the input field.

When to Use

  • change → Immediate validation.
  • blur → Better UX for email, username or async validation.
  • submit → Validate the entire form only when the user submits it.

Common Interview Questions

  • What is the default value of updateOn? change.
  • Why use blur? To avoid validating on every keystroke.
  • When is submit useful? For large forms where validation should occur only during submission.

How to say it in an interview

"The updateOn option controls when Angular updates form values and runs validation. The default is change, which validates on every input change. blur waits until the field loses focus, while submit delays validation until the form is submitted. Using blur or submit can improve performance and user experience, especially with async validators."

question: What is a selector in Angular and why use it?
answer:
The selector is a property in the @Component decorator that defines how the component is identified in a template. It acts like a custom HTML tag, so Angular knows where to render the component.

Selectors can be an element (app-user), an attribute ([appHighlight]), or a class.
tip: Prefix your selectors (app-…) so they never clash with real or future HTML elements.
code:
@Component({ selector: 'app-user', templateUrl: './user.html' })
// used as: <app-user></app-user>

question: What is the entry point of an Angular application?
answer: main.ts is the entry point. It bootstraps the app — historically platformBrowserDynamic().bootstrapModule(AppModule), and in modern standalone Angular bootstrapApplication(AppComponent). This initialises the root and renders the app into the browser.
tip: Bootstrapping = 'start the app and render the root component'. Know both the module and standalone forms.
code:
// modern standalone bootstrap
bootstrapApplication(AppComponent, { providers: [provideRouter(routes)] });

question: What is the use of router-outlet?
answer: <router-outlet> is a placeholder in a template where the router renders the component that matches the current route. As the URL changes, Angular swaps the component shown at that outlet. You can also have named/nested outlets.
tip: Think of router-outlet as the 'stage' where routed components are displayed.
code:
<nav>…</nav>
<router-outlet></router-outlet> <!-- routed component renders here -->

question: How do you call a REST API in Angular?
answer: Use the HttpClient service (provide it with provideHttpClient()). Wrap the calls in a dedicated service that returns Observables, and handle the HTTP verbs (GET/POST/PUT/DELETE) with proper error handling via catchError. Components subscribe (ideally through the async pipe).
tip: Keep API calls in a service, not the component — it's reusable, testable, and mockable.
deep:
What is HttpClient?

HttpClient is Angular's built-in service for communicating with REST APIs. It supports all HTTP methods such as GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, and DELETE. Every request returns an Observable, allowing asynchronous handling, retries, cancellation, and RxJS operators.

Typical Flow

Component
     ↓
UserService
     ↓
HttpClient
     ↓
REST API
     ↓
Database

The component should never call HttpClient directly. Instead, API calls are encapsulated inside a service to keep the application modular, reusable, and easy to test.

1. Configure HttpClient

In standalone Angular applications, enable it using provideHttpClient().

bootstrapApplication(AppComponent, {
  providers: [provideHttpClient()]
});

2. Create a Service

@Injectable({ providedIn: 'root' })
export class UserService {
  constructor(private http: HttpClient) {}

  getUsers() {
    return this.http.get<User[]>('/api/users');
  }

  createUser(user: User) {
    return this.http.post<User>('/api/users', user);
  }
}

3. Consume in Component

export class UsersComponent {
  users$ = this.userService.getUsers();

  constructor(private userService: UserService) {}
}
<ul>
  <li *ngFor="let user of users$ | async">
    {{ user.name }}
  </li>
</ul>

Using the async pipe automatically subscribes and unsubscribes from the Observable.

HTTP Methods

MethodPurpose
GETRetrieve data
POSTCreate new resource
PUTReplace existing resource
PATCHUpdate part of a resource
DELETERemove resource

Error Handling

Use RxJS operators like catchError to handle server errors in the service instead of the component.

getUsers() {
  return this.http.get<User[]>('/api/users').pipe(
    catchError(err => {
      console.error(err);
      return throwError(() => err);
    })
  );
}

Real-world Example

Imagine a Customer Management application.

  1. The user opens the Customer page.
  2. The component calls customerService.getCustomers().
  3. The service sends a GET request using HttpClient.
  4. The backend returns customer data.
  5. The Observable emits the response.
  6. The UI updates automatically using the async pipe.
CustomerComponent
        ↓
CustomerService
        ↓
GET /api/customers
        ↓
Spring Boot API
        ↓
PostgreSQL

Http Interceptors

Enterprise applications rarely attach headers in every service method. Instead, an HttpInterceptor automatically adds common information such as JWT tokens, correlation IDs, or logs every request.

export const authInterceptor: HttpInterceptorFn = (req, next) => {
  const token = localStorage.getItem('token');

  const authReq = req.clone({
    setHeaders: {
      Authorization: `Bearer ${token}`
    }
  });

  return next(authReq);
};

Best Practices

  • Keep all API calls inside services.
  • Return Observables instead of subscribing inside services.
  • Use the async pipe whenever possible.
  • Handle errors with catchError.
  • Use Interceptors for authentication headers and common request logic.
  • Strongly type API responses using TypeScript interfaces.

Interview Answer

"In Angular, REST APIs are called using HttpClient. We usually create a dedicated service that returns Observables and keeps HTTP logic separate from the UI. Components consume these Observables using the async pipe or subscribe when necessary. Error handling is done with RxJS operators like catchError, and enterprise applications typically use Http Interceptors to attach authentication tokens and handle common request logic."

question: How do you make an API call that depends on another call's response?
answer: Chain them with switchMap so the second request uses the first's result (and stale first-calls are cancelled). For independent parallel calls use forkJoin; for ordered writes use concatMap.
tip: Dependent = switchMap/concatMap; independent-parallel = forkJoin.

question: What is the difference between a component and a directive?
answer: A component is a directive with a template — it controls a view. A directive has no template; it adds behaviour or changes the appearance/structure of existing elements (attribute directives like ngClass, structural directives like *ngIf).
tip: One-liner: 'A component is a directive with a template.'
deep:
The relationship

All three are directives — a component is just the special case that owns a view:

TypeTemplate?Purpose
Componentyescontrols a piece of UI (view + logic)
Attribute directivenochanges appearance/behaviour of an existing element
Structural directivenoadds/removes elements from the DOM

Component

@Component({ selector: 'app-user', template: '<h2>{{ name }}</h2>' })
export class UserComponent { name = 'Asha'; }

Attribute directive — behaviour, no template

@Directive({ selector: '[appHighlight]' })
export class HighlightDirective {
  constructor(el: ElementRef) { el.nativeElement.style.background = 'yellow'; }
}
// <p appHighlight>...</p>

Structural directive — changes DOM layout

*ngIf, *ngFor (and the v17 @if/@for) add or remove elements. The * is sugar for wrapping the element in an <ng-template>.

How to say it in an interview

“A component is a directive with a template — it renders a view. A directive has no template; attribute directives change how an existing element looks or behaves, and structural directives add or remove elements. Every component is a directive, but not every directive is a component.”

question: Why do we use services in Angular?
answer:
• Encapsulate reusable business logic and API calls.
  • Share data/state between components (often via a BehaviorSubject).
  • Provide a single instance (singleton) through dependency injection.
  • Enforce separation of concerns — keep components focused on the view.
tip: Components render; services do the work. Keep logic out of components.

question: What are Angular route guards and their types?
answer:
Guards control access to and navigation between routes.

  • CanActivate — allow/deny entering a route (auth).
  • CanDeactivate — confirm leaving (e.g. unsaved-changes prompt).
  • Resolve — pre-fetch data before the route activates.
  • CanMatch / CanLoad — control whether a lazy route is even loaded.
tip: For auth, a CanActivate guard returning a UrlTree is the clean redirect-to-login pattern.
code:
export const authGuard: CanActivateFn = () =>
  inject(AuthService).isLoggedIn() || inject(Router).createUrlTree(['/login']);
deep:
The guard types

GuardRunsTypical use
CanActivatebefore entering a routeauth / role check
CanActivateChildbefore entering any child routeprotect a whole feature area
CanDeactivatebefore leaving a route“You have unsaved changes” prompt
CanMatchbefore a route is even matchedfeature flags, role-based route swapping, block lazy load
Resolveafter activation is allowed, before renderpre-fetch data

Note: CanLoad is deprecated in favour of CanMatch, which also blocks the lazy chunk from loading.

Return types

A guard returns boolean, a UrlTree (redirect), or an Observable/Promise of those. Returning a UrlTree is the clean way to redirect:

export const authGuard: CanActivateFn = (route, state) => {
  const auth = inject(AuthService);
  const router = inject(Router);
  return auth.isLoggedIn()
    ? true
    : router.createUrlTree(['/login'], { queryParams: { returnUrl: state.url } });
};

Functional guards (modern) vs class guards (legacy)

Since v14, guards are plain functions (CanActivateFn) using inject() — less boilerplate than the old class-based implements CanActivate. Wire them in the route config:

{ path: 'admin', canActivate: [authGuard], loadComponent: ... }
{ path: 'edit', canDeactivate: [unsavedChangesGuard] }

CanDeactivate pattern

export const unsavedChangesGuard: CanDeactivateFn<EditComponent> =
  (component) => component.form.pristine || confirm('Discard changes?');

How to say it in an interview

“Guards gate navigation. CanActivate/CanActivateChild protect entering routes, CanDeactivate confirms leaving, CanMatch decides whether a route (including its lazy bundle) is used at all, and Resolve pre-fetches data. Modern guards are functions using inject(), and returning a UrlTree is the idiomatic redirect.”

question: How would you debug "Cannot read properties of undefined" in Angular?
answer:
It means you accessed a property on something that isn't ready/loaded yet (often async data rendered before it arrives). Fixes:

  • Guard the template with *ngIf="data" or the safe-navigation operator data?.name.
  • Initialise the variable before use; check the async flow with the async pipe.
  • Use the browser devtools/stack trace to find the exact line and inspect the value.
tip: The template safe-navigation operator (?.) prevents this for async data that hasn't loaded yet.
code:
{{ user?.profile?.name }}
{{ u.name }}

question: What are the options for state management in Angular?
answer:
• Service + BehaviorSubject — simple shared state for small/medium apps.
  • Signals — modern, fine-grained local/shared state.
  • NgRx / NgRx SignalStore — structured Redux-style store for large apps needing traceability and DevTools.
tip: Match the tool to the app size — don't reach for NgRx on a small app.
deep:
The spectrum, smallest to largest

OptionGood forTrade-off
Component state / @Inputlocal, single componentdoesn't scale to shared state
Service + BehaviorSubjectsmall–medium shared statemanual, boilerplate for complex flows
Service + Signalsmodern shared/local statenewer; async still needs RxJS
NgRx SignalStorestructured store, less boilerplate than classic NgRxlearning curve
NgRx (Store/Effects)large apps needing traceability, DevTools, time-travelmost boilerplate

Service + BehaviorSubject — the workhorse

@Injectable({ providedIn: 'root' })
export class CartService {
  private items$ = new BehaviorSubject<Item[]>([]);
  readonly items = this.items$.asObservable();
  add(i: Item) { this.items$.next([...this.items$.value, i]); }
}

Expose a read-only stream, mutate through methods. This covers a surprising amount of real-world apps.

Signal-based store

@Injectable({ providedIn: 'root' })
export class CounterStore {
  private _count = signal(0);
  count = this._count.asReadonly();
  double = computed(() => this._count() * 2);
  inc() { this._count.update(n => n + 1); }
}

When NgRx earns its keep

Reach for NgRx when many components share complex state, you need an audit trail of how state changed (actions), time-travel debugging, or well-defined side-effect handling (Effects). Its cost is boilerplate — actions, reducers, selectors, effects — so it's overkill for small apps.

How to say it in an interview

“I scale the tool to the app. Local state stays in the component; shared state goes in a service with a BehaviorSubject or signals. I only introduce NgRx (or SignalStore) when the app is large enough to need structured actions, traceability, and DevTools — otherwise it’s just boilerplate.”

question: constructor vs ngOnInit — what's the difference?
answer:
The constructor is a TypeScript/JS class feature that runs when the class is instantiated — Angular uses it only to inject dependencies. ngOnInit is an Angular lifecycle hook that runs after the first change detection, once @Input values are set.

Rule of thumb: inject in the constructor, initialise in ngOnInit (data fetching, setup that needs inputs).
tip: @Input values are undefined in the constructor — that alone is why real init belongs in ngOnInit.
code:
constructor(private api: ApiService) {}   // DI only
ngOnInit() { this.user = this.api.getUser(this.id); } // id is ready here
deep:
Why two places at all?

The constructor fires the instant Angular creates the component object — before it has wired up bindings or rendered anything. So @Input properties are still undefined and the DOM/@ViewChild don't exist yet. ngOnInit runs a beat later, after the first ngOnChanges, so inputs are populated.

constructorngOnInit
Belongs tothe class (TS)Angular lifecycle
Runson instantiationafter first ngOnChanges
@Input ready?noyes
Use fordependency injectioninit logic, data fetch
@Input() id!: string;
constructor(private api: ApiService) {
  console.log(this.id); // undefined 😖
}
ngOnInit() {
  console.log(this.id); // 'abc' ✅
  this.api.getUser(this.id).subscribe(u => this.user = u);
}

How to say it in an interview

“The constructor is a class feature Angular uses for dependency injection — inputs aren’t set yet. ngOnInit runs after the first change detection when inputs are available, so I do initialization and data fetching there.”

question: What is trackBy in *ngFor and why use it?
answer: By default *ngFor tracks list items by object identity, so if the array reference changes (e.g. after an HTTP refresh) Angular destroys and recreates every DOM node. A trackBy function tells Angular how to identify an item (usually by id), so it only re-renders the rows that actually changed.
tip: trackBy is the fix for a list that flickers or loses input focus when the data reloads.
code:
• {{ u.name }}
    • trackById(index: number, u: User) { return u.id; } // v17 control flow: @for (u of users; track u.id) { ... }
deep:
The problem it solves

When you reassign a list (this.users = [...fresh]), the new objects have new references. Without trackBy, Angular can't tell they represent the same rows, so it tears down and rebuilds the whole list — expensive, and it drops DOM state like scroll position, focus, or CSS animations.

How trackBy fixes it

Return a stable unique key per item. Angular then diffs by that key and touches only added/removed/changed rows.

trackById(index: number, item: User): number {
  return item.id;
}

In the v17+ control flow, track is mandatory: @for (u of users; track u.id) { }.

How to say it in an interview

“trackBy gives each item a stable identity so *ngFor reuses DOM nodes instead of rebuilding the whole list when the array reference changes — a big win for large or frequently-refreshed lists.”

question: forRoot() vs forChild() — what's the difference?
answer:
forRoot() is called once in the root module to register singleton services and application-wide configuration. forChild() is used in feature modules to register routes or components without creating another instance of those services.

A common example is RouterModule.forRoot() in the root module and RouterModule.forChild() in feature modules.
tip: Use forRoot() only once. Calling it multiple times can create duplicate singleton services.
deep:
What is the forRoot()/forChild() Pattern?

The forRoot()/forChild() pattern prevents singleton services from being created multiple times when a module is imported into different feature modules.

forRoot()

  • Called once in the root module.
  • Registers providers.
  • Creates singleton services.
  • Can accept application-wide configuration.

forChild()

  • Used in feature modules.
  • Does not register singleton providers.
  • Typically registers routes or declarations.

Example

// Root module
RouterModule.forRoot(routes)

// Feature module
RouterModule.forChild(featureRoutes)

Difference

forRoot()forChild()
Called once.Can be called in multiple feature modules.
Registers providers.Does not create singleton providers.
Application-wide configuration.Feature-specific configuration.

Common Interview Questions

  • Why do we use forRoot()? To register singleton services only once.
  • Why not use forRoot() in every module? It can create duplicate service instances.
  • Where is this pattern commonly used? RouterModule.
  • Is it still important with Standalone Components? It mainly applies to NgModule-based applications. Standalone APIs usually configure providers with functions such as provideRouter().

How to say it in an interview

"The forRoot()/forChild() pattern is used in NgModule-based Angular applications. forRoot() registers singleton services and is called once in the root module, while forChild() is used in feature modules without recreating those services. A common example is RouterModule.forRoot() and RouterModule.forChild(). In modern standalone applications, this pattern is largely replaced by provider functions like provideRouter()."

question: What causes ExpressionChangedAfterItHasBeenCheckedError?
answer: In dev mode Angular runs change detection twice and compares. If a bound value changed between the two passes — usually because you mutated it in a lifecycle hook like ngAfterViewInit that runs after the view was already checked — it throws this error to warn of an inconsistent view.
tip: It only appears in dev mode — but it signals a real bug: your view and model briefly disagreed.
deep:
Why it happens

To guarantee the rendered DOM matches the model, dev-mode Angular does a second change-detection pass right after the first and checks that nothing changed. If a value updated in between, the two passes disagree and Angular throws — because in production (single pass) the user would see a stale value.

Typical trigger

@ViewChild('box') box!: ElementRef;
ngAfterViewInit() {
  this.height = this.box.nativeElement.offsetHeight; // bound in the template
} // ❌ view was already checked this cycle

Fixes

  • Move the change to an earlier hook (ngOnInit) if possible.
  • Defer it to the next tick: setTimeout(() => this.height = h) or Promise.resolve().then(...).
  • Force a re-check: inject ChangeDetectorRef and call detectChanges() after updating.

How to say it in an interview

“It’s a dev-mode guard: Angular checks the view twice and a bound value changed after the first check — usually from updating state in ngAfterViewInit. I fix it by moving the update earlier, deferring it a tick, or calling detectChanges().”

question: Observable vs Promise — what's the difference?
answer:
• Promise — a single future value; eager (runs immediately); not cancellable; no operators.
  • Observable — a stream of 0..∞ values over time; lazy (runs only on subscribe); cancellable via unsubscribe; rich operators (map, switchMap, debounceTime).

Angular's HttpClient returns Observables, which is why they dominate Angular apps.
tip: Say the three big wins: multiple values, lazy, and cancellable — plus operators.
deep:
Side-by-side

PromiseObservable
Valuesexactly onezero to many, over time
Eager/lazyeager (runs on creation)lazy (runs on subscribe)
Cancelnoyes (unsubscribe)
Operatorsnonemap, filter, switchMap…
Retrymanualretry() operator

Lazy matters

A Promise starts its work the moment it's created. An Observable does nothing until something subscribes — so an unsubscribed HTTP Observable never even fires the request. That laziness is what enables cancellation and operators like switchMap (cancel the previous request when a new one starts).

// typeahead: cancel stale requests automatically
this.query$.pipe(
  debounceTime(300),
  switchMap(q => this.http.get(`/search?q=${q}`))
).subscribe(...);

Interop

Convert with firstValueFrom(obs) (Observable→Promise) or from(promise) (Promise→Observable).

How to say it in an interview

“A Promise resolves once, eagerly, and can’t be cancelled. An Observable is a lazy, cancellable stream of many values with a big operator library — which is why Angular’s HttpClient and reactive forms use Observables.”

question: How would you handle rapid user actions (e.g. live search or AI analysis requests) to avoid multiple API calls and outdated results?
answer: Use debounceTime() to wait until the user stops typing or clicking for a short duration, reducing unnecessary API calls. Then use switchMap() to automatically cancel any previous pending request whenever a new request is triggered. This ensures that only the latest response is processed and displayed.
tip: Remember: debounceTime delays the request, switchMap cancels the previous request.
deep:
Real-world scenario

Imagine an AI dashboard where the user clicks different timestamps on a video to request AI analysis. Each click triggers an HTTP request.

If the user clicks Timestamp A, then immediately clicks Timestamp B, you don't want the response from A arriving after B and overwriting the latest result.

switchMap() automatically unsubscribes from (cancels) the previous HTTP request and subscribes only to the latest one, ensuring stale data is ignored.

Role of debounceTime()

debounceTime(300) waits until the user pauses for 300ms before sending the request. This prevents flooding the server with unnecessary API calls during rapid typing or clicking.

Why switchMap?

OperatorBehavior
switchMapCancels previous request and keeps only the latest.
mergeMapRuns all requests concurrently.
concatMapQueues requests and executes one after another.
exhaustMapIgnores new requests until the current one finishes.

How to answer in an interview

“For rapid user interactions like live search or AI analysis, I'd combine debounceTime() and switchMap(). debounceTime reduces unnecessary API calls, while switchMap automatically cancels previous requests so only the latest response updates the UI. This prevents race conditions and keeps the frontend responsive.”

question: Your page displays 10,000 records and scrolling becomes very slow. How would you optimize it?
answer: First, use trackBy so Angular reuses existing DOM elements instead of recreating them. For very large datasets, use CDK Virtual Scroll so only the visible items are rendered. Combine this with ChangeDetectionStrategy.OnPush to reduce unnecessary change detection.
tip: The three keywords interviewers expect are: trackBy, Virtual Scroll, and OnPush.
code:
{{ user.name }}
trackById(index: number, user: User) { return user.id; }
deep:
Real-world scenario

A dashboard loads thousands of customer records. Without optimization Angular creates thousands of DOM elements, making scrolling slow.

Solution

  • Use trackBy to reuse DOM elements.
  • Use CDK Virtual Scroll to render only visible rows.
  • Use OnPush to reduce change detection.

Interview answer

"I first reduce DOM creation using trackBy. If the dataset is huge, I implement Angular CDK Virtual Scroll so only visible rows exist in the DOM. Finally, I use OnPush to minimize unnecessary change detection."

question: How do you prevent memory leaks caused by RxJS subscriptions?
answer: Always unsubscribe from long-lived Observables when the component is destroyed. In modern Angular, the recommended approach is using takeUntilDestroyed(). In older versions, use takeUntil() with a Subject or rely on the async pipe whenever possible.
tip: The Async Pipe automatically subscribes and unsubscribes for you.
deep:
Real-world scenario

A component subscribes to WebSocket updates. Every time the user navigates away and back, another subscription is created, causing duplicate events and increasing memory usage.

Best practices

  • Prefer the Async Pipe in templates.
  • Use takeUntilDestroyed() for manual subscriptions.
  • Avoid storing unnecessary subscriptions.

Interview answer

"I avoid memory leaks by preferring the Async Pipe. For manual subscriptions I use takeUntilDestroyed(), ensuring Angular automatically cleans them up when the component is destroyed."

question: How would you prevent users from submitting the same form multiple times by clicking the Save button repeatedly?
answer: Disable the Save button while the request is in progress or use exhaustMap(). It ignores subsequent button clicks until the current request completes, preventing duplicate submissions.
tip: Remember: switchMap cancels previous, exhaustMap ignores new requests.
deep:
Real-world scenario

A user clicks Save five times because the API is slow. Without protection, five identical orders may be created.

Solution

  • Disable the Save button.
  • Or use exhaustMap() so additional clicks are ignored until the first request completes.

Interview answer

"For Save buttons I usually combine a loading state with exhaustMap(), ensuring duplicate submissions are ignored while the current request is running."

question: How would you share data between unrelated Angular components?
answer: For unrelated components, avoid directly referencing each other. Use a shared service with an RxJS BehaviorSubject or use NgRx if the state is shared across multiple features. Parent-child communication should still use @Input() and @Output().
tip: The communication mechanism depends on the relationship between components.
deep:
Communication choices

ScenarioRecommended Approach
Parent → Child@Input()
Child → Parent@Output()
Unrelated ComponentsShared Service + BehaviorSubject
Large ApplicationNgRx Store

Interview answer

"I choose the communication mechanism based on the relationship between components. Parent-child uses Input/Output, unrelated components use a shared service, and application-wide shared state uses NgRx."

question: Your Angular application has become slow because the initial bundle size is very large. How would you improve the loading performance?
answer: I would split the application into feature modules and lazy load them using the Angular Router. This ensures only the modules required for the current route are downloaded initially, reducing the bundle size and improving the application's startup time.
tip: The first optimization interviewers expect is Lazy Loading.
deep:
Real-world scenario

Your application has 15 modules, but users usually access only 2 or 3 of them. Loading everything during startup increases the bundle size and slows down the application.

Solution

  • Use Lazy Loading for feature modules.
  • Preload frequently used modules if necessary.
  • Keep the initial bundle as small as possible.

Interview answer

"I split the application into feature modules and lazy load them using the router. This reduces the initial bundle size, improves startup time, and downloads features only when users navigate to them."

question: Multiple components call the same API, causing duplicate network requests. How would you optimize it?
answer: I would cache the API response inside a shared service using shareReplay(1). The first component triggers the HTTP request, while subsequent subscribers receive the cached response without making additional network calls.
tip: shareReplay(1) is the most common RxJS operator used for HTTP caching.
deep:
Real-world scenario

The Header, Dashboard and Profile components all request the logged-in user. Without caching, three identical HTTP requests are sent.

Solution

Store the Observable in a service and use shareReplay(1) so every component receives the same cached result.

Interview answer

"I'd cache the HTTP Observable using shareReplay(1), ensuring the API is called only once while all components share the same response."

question: How would you prevent unauthenticated users from accessing protected pages?
answer: I would use an Angular Route Guard such as CanActivate. Before navigation, the guard checks whether the user is authenticated. If not, it redirects the user to the login page.
tip: Authentication checks belong in Route Guards, not inside every component.
deep:
Real-world scenario

A user manually enters the URL '/admin'. Even though the menu is hidden, they should still be prevented from accessing the page.

Solution

Use a Route Guard to verify authentication before navigation.

Interview answer

"Instead of checking authentication inside every component, I centralize the logic using a Route Guard, ensuring protected routes cannot be accessed directly."

question: A component is re-rendering frequently and performance is poor. How would you optimize Angular Change Detection?
answer: I would use ChangeDetectionStrategy.OnPush. With OnPush, Angular checks the component only when an @Input() reference changes, an event occurs, or an Observable emits. This significantly reduces unnecessary change detection cycles.
tip: OnPush works best with immutable objects and the Async Pipe.
deep:
Real-world scenario

A dashboard contains many widgets that refresh frequently. Default change detection causes every component to be checked after each event, reducing performance.

Solution

  • Use OnPush.
  • Avoid mutating objects directly.
  • Prefer immutable updates and the Async Pipe.

Interview answer

"I'd switch the component to OnPush so Angular performs change detection only when necessary. Combined with immutable data and the Async Pipe, this greatly improves rendering performance."

question: How would you implement an auto-save feature for a form without sending an API request on every keystroke?
answer: I would subscribe to form.valueChanges, apply debounceTime() to wait until the user stops typing, use distinctUntilChanged() to avoid duplicate requests, and finally use switchMap() to cancel any pending save request when new changes occur.
tip: Auto-save is one of the best real-world examples combining debounceTime, distinctUntilChanged and switchMap.
deep:
Real-world scenario

While writing a document or filling a long form, users expect their work to be saved automatically without clicking a Save button.

Solution

  • Listen to valueChanges.
  • Wait until the user pauses typing.
  • Ignore duplicate values.
  • Cancel previous save requests if new edits occur.

Interview answer

"I'd use valueChanges with debounceTime(), distinctUntilChanged() and switchMap() so drafts are saved efficiently without flooding the server."

question: How would you handle HTTP errors consistently across the entire Angular application?
answer: I would create an HTTP Interceptor. It centralizes error handling, logging, authentication failures, and token refresh logic instead of duplicating the same code inside every component or service.
tip: Cross-cutting concerns like authentication and error handling belong in an Interceptor.
deep:
Real-world scenario

Every API call should display consistent error messages and automatically redirect users when a 401 Unauthorized response is received.

Interview answer

"Instead of handling errors in every component, I'd centralize them using an HTTP Interceptor. This keeps the code cleaner and ensures consistent behavior throughout the application."

question: How would you handle an expired JWT token without forcing the user to log in again?
answer: I would detect a 401 Unauthorized response inside an HTTP Interceptor, call the Refresh Token API, store the new access token, retry the original request, and only redirect to login if the refresh request also fails.
tip: The retry logic should be implemented inside an HTTP Interceptor, not in every API call.
code:
401 Error
↓
Refresh Token API
↓
New Access Token
↓
Retry Original Request
deep:
Real-world scenario

A user's access token expires after one hour, but the refresh token is still valid. The user should continue working without interruption.

Interview answer

"I'd intercept 401 responses, refresh the token transparently, retry the failed request, and only redirect to login if refreshing the token also fails."

question: How would you display upload progress while uploading a large file?
answer: Angular's HttpClient supports upload progress events. By enabling reportProgress and observe: 'events', I can calculate the upload percentage and display a progress bar to the user.
tip: Always use upload progress for large files to improve user experience.
deep:
Real-world scenario

A user uploads a 500 MB video. Without progress feedback, the application appears frozen and users may retry the upload.

Interview answer

"I'd use HttpClient's progress events to calculate the upload percentage and display a progress bar, giving users clear feedback during long uploads."

question: What are microfrontends and how do they differ from a normal frontend?
answer:
A microfrontend architecture splits one web app into independently built, tested and deployed pieces, each owned end-to-end by a different team, and composed together in the browser at runtime.

A normal (monolithic) frontend is one codebase, one build, one deploy — every team's change ships in the same bundle, on the same release train.

The real difference isn't technical, it's organisational: microfrontends trade runtime and tooling complexity for independent deployability. In Angular this is usually done with Module Federation (Webpack 5 / Native Federation), where a shell loads remotes at runtime.
tip: The follow-up is always 'when would you NOT use them?' — if you have one team and one release train, microfrontends are pure cost with no benefit. Say that out loud; it's the senior signal.
deep:
Monolith vs microfrontend

Monolithic frontendMicrofrontends
Buildoneone per remote
Deployall teams togetherindependently
Reposingle (or Nx monorepo)often one per team
Tech choiceone Angular versioncan differ (at a cost)
Blast radiusa bad merge blocks everyoneisolated to one remote
Runtime costnoneversion skew, duplicate deps
Best for1–3 teamsmany teams, one product

The problem they actually solve

Not performance — team autonomy. At ~5+ teams in one repo, the release train becomes the bottleneck: your finished feature waits on someone else's failing test, merge conflicts multiply, and a regression anywhere blocks every deploy. Microfrontends let the Orders team ship at 2pm without asking the Payments team.

If you don't have that pain, you don't have the problem they solve.

How composition works

  • Build-time (npm packages) — simple, but a remote change needs a shell rebuild + redeploy. That defeats the point.
  • Runtime via Module Federation ← the standard answer. The shell fetches remoteEntry.js at runtime, so the remote deploys on its own.
  • iframes — bulletproof isolation, painful UX (routing, sizing, modals, auth).
  • Web Components — framework-agnostic wrapper, often combined with the above.

The hard parts (this is where interviews go)

1. Shared dependencies. Three remotes each bundling Angular means shipping it three times. Mark framework packages singleton: true so one copy is shared — but then all remotes must agree on a compatible version, which quietly re-couples the teams you just decoupled.

2. The Router must be a singleton. Two Angular Router instances fighting over the URL is the classic microfrontend bug.

3. Shared state & auth. Don't share a store across remotes — that's coupling by the back door. Keep the token in the shell (or a cookie) and pass context down via inputs/custom events. Cross-remote communication should be coarse events, not shared objects.

4. Version skew. The shell and remotes are deployed at different times, so any combination can be live simultaneously. Contracts must be backward compatible.

5. Consistent UX. Independent teams drift visually — a shared design system (published as a library) is mandatory, not optional.

In Angular specifically

ng add @angular-architects/module-federation --project shell   --port 4200
ng add @angular-architects/module-federation --project orders  --port 4201

For modern Angular (esbuild/Vite builder), Webpack Module Federation doesn't apply — use Native Federation (@angular-architects/native-federation), which is the same idea on browser-native ES modules and import maps.

When NOT to use them

  • One or two teams — you get every cost and no benefit. An Nx monorepo with enforced module boundaries gives you most of the structure with none of the runtime pain.
  • A tightly-coupled UI where remotes constantly share state — you'd build a distributed monolith: all the complexity, none of the independence.
  • Performance-critical pages — duplicate deps and extra round-trips make bundles bigger, not smaller.

How to say it in an interview

“Microfrontends split the app into independently deployable pieces owned by different teams, composed at runtime — in Angular, usually Module Federation with a shell loading remotes. The point isn't performance, it's removing the shared release train once you have enough teams that it's a bottleneck. The costs are real though: framework packages have to be shared singletons, the Router must be a singleton, and since shell and remotes deploy independently you have to treat their contracts as backward-compatible APIs. For one or two teams I'd use an Nx monorepo with enforced boundaries instead — same structure, no runtime cost.”

question: How do you build a scalable enterprise Angular application?
answer:
First, define the axis — this is the trap. On a frontend, "scalable" almost never means server traffic; it means the codebase and the team can keep growing without the app rotting.

  • Code scale — feature-based structure with enforced boundaries, so 200k lines stay navigable.
  • Team scale — clear ownership; teams don't collide in the same files.
  • Runtime scale — lazy loading, OnPush, virtual scrolling, performance budgets so it stays fast as features pile on.
  • Data scale — pagination and virtualisation; never render 10k rows.
tip: Ask 'scalable in what sense?' or state your axis explicitly. Candidates who drift into load balancers and DB sharding for a FRONTEND question expose that the resume bullet was filler.
deep:
Step 1 — disambiguate, don't ramble

“Scalable” is the vaguest word on most resumes, so the interviewer is checking whether you know what you meant. Name the axis first. For an enterprise Angular app the honest answer is usually: the codebase and the number of teams touching it — plus keeping runtime performance flat as it grows.

Code scale — structure by feature, not by type

// Doesn't scale — every feature dumps into shared folders
src/app/components/  services/  models/

// Scales — a feature is a deletable, ownable unit
src/app/
  core/                 # singletons: auth, interceptors, guards
  shared/               # dumb reusable UI, pipes
  features/
    orders/             # everything Orders, nothing else imports its internals
      data-access/
      feature-list/
      ui/

The test: can I delete this folder and only this feature breaks? If deleting Orders breaks Billing, you don't have a boundary.

Boundaries have to be enforced by tooling

A structure convention decays the moment someone is in a hurry. In an Nx monorepo, tag libraries and let @nx/enforce-module-boundaries fail the build on a cross-feature import. That converts “please don't import that” into a lint error — the difference between an architecture and a wish.

Team scale

  • CODEOWNERS per feature folder — reviews route automatically.
  • A shared design system as its own library, or every team reinvents the button.
  • Nx affected — only rebuild/retest what changed, so CI doesn't grow linearly with the repo.
  • Microfrontends only when the shared release train genuinely becomes the bottleneck (see microfrontends).

Runtime scale — what actually degrades

GrowsSymptomFix
Featuresinitial bundle bloatslazy-loaded routes, @defer, budgets in angular.json
Components on screenevery CD cycle checks everythingOnPush + signals
Rows of dataDOM chokesvirtual scroll + trackBy, server-side paging
Subscriptionsmemory leak, slow over timetakeUntilDestroyed / async pipe
// A budget makes bundle regressions fail CI instead of shipping
"budgets": [{ "type": "initial", "maximumError": "1mb" }]

State — the thing that rots first

Don't reach for a global store reflexively. Keep state as local as possible; promote to a store only when genuinely shared. A store that every feature writes to is a global variable with ceremony — and it re-couples the features you carefully separated.

What makes it "enterprise"

Usually the non-functional requirements, and they're worth naming: role-based access control, audit trails, i18n/l10n, accessibility compliance, SSO, long-term browser support, and release processes with sign-off. Mentioning these shows you've worked on real enterprise software rather than a large CRUD app.

How to say it in an interview

“First I'd clarify the axis — on the frontend, scaling usually means the codebase and the team, not traffic. I structure by feature so each one is a deletable unit, and enforce that with Nx module-boundary lint rules so it's checked by CI rather than by convention. For runtime I lazy-load routes, use OnPush/signals, virtualise long lists, and set bundle budgets so a regression fails the build. And I keep state local by default — a global store everything writes to quietly re-couples the features you just isolated.”

question: Walk me through the architecture of your Angular application.
answer:
Answer in layers, and give a reason for each choice — the interviewer is testing whether you designed it or inherited it.

  • Structure — core/ (singletons: auth, interceptors, error handling), shared/ (dumb reusable UI), features/ (one lazy-loaded folder per domain).
  • Components — smart/container components fetch data; dumb/presentational ones take @Input and emit @Output, so they're trivially testable.
  • Services + DI — HTTP and business logic live in services, never components.
  • State — local first; a store only where state is genuinely shared.
  • Cross-cutting — interceptors for auth/errors, guards for routes.
tip: Every 'what' needs a 'why' with a trade-off. 'We used NgRx because it's standard' is a fail; 'we used NgRx because 4 features shared server state and we needed the devtools time-travel to debug it' is a pass.
code:
src/app/
  core/            # provided once: interceptors, guards, auth
  shared/          # dumb UI, pipes, directives — no feature imports
  features/
    orders/
      data-access/   # OrdersService, models
      feature-list/  # smart component (routed)
      ui/            # dumb components
  app.routes.ts    # lazy: loadChildren / loadComponent
deep:
How to structure the answer

Don't list files. Go top-down: what the app does → how code is organised → how data flows → cross-cutting concerns → trade-offs you'd revisit. Two minutes, then stop and let them drill.

1. Folder structure — and the rule behind it

LayerHoldsRule
core/auth, interceptors, global error handlingprovided once, app-wide
shared/dumb UI, pipes, directivesmust not import from features/
features/<domain>/everything for one domainlazy-loaded; no cross-feature imports

The invariant worth stating: dependencies point inward (features → shared → core), never sideways between features. If Orders imports from Billing, they should be one feature or share a library.

2. Modules or standalone?

Standalone components are the default from Angular 17+ — no NgModule boilerplate, and loadComponent lazy-loads a single route. If your app is NgModule-based, say so and say why (legacy, gradual migration) rather than pretending. Knowing both and having a migration opinion is the strong answer.

// standalone, lazy — the modern default
{ path: 'orders', loadComponent: () => import('./features/orders/list.component') }

// NgModule-based lazy route
{ path: 'orders', loadChildren: () => import('./orders/orders.module').then(m => m.OrdersModule) }

3. Smart vs dumb components

// Smart — knows about services, no template logic worth testing
@Component({ template: `<order-table [orders]="orders$ | async" (select)="open($event)" />` })
export class OrdersPage {
  private svc = inject(OrdersService);
  orders$ = this.svc.getOrders();
}

// Dumb — inputs/outputs only, OnPush, trivial to test and reuse
@Component({ changeDetection: ChangeDetectionStrategy.OnPush })
export class OrderTable {
  @Input() orders: Order[] | null = null;
  @Output() select = new EventEmitter<Order>();
}

Why it matters: dumb components have no dependencies to mock, so testing them is a function call. It's also what makes OnPush safe.

4. Where logic lives

  • Component — view state only.
  • Service — HTTP, business rules, orchestration. If a component has a HttpClient call in it, that's a smell.
  • Store — shared state across features.

Be ready for “why not put it in the component?” — because services are injectable, mockable, and reusable, and because a component's job is rendering.

5. State management — have an opinion

The honest senior answer is a ladder, not a religion: component state → a service with a BehaviorSubject/signal → a full store (NgRx/NgRx Signal Store) only when several features share server state and you need devtools/time-travel to reason about it. NgRx on a small app is boilerplate for its own sake — say that; it shows judgement.

6. Cross-cutting concerns

  • HTTP interceptors — attach the auth token, refresh on 401, map errors centrally.
  • Route guards — auth/role checks (CanActivate).
  • Global ErrorHandler — one place that logs to Sentry.

7. Close with a trade-off

Volunteer one thing you'd change: “we started NgModule-based and are migrating to standalone,” or “our shared/ folder became a junk drawer — I'd split it into ui/ and utils/.” Naming a weakness in your own architecture reads as seniority, not weakness — it proves you evaluated it rather than inherited it.

How to say it in an interview

“It's a lazy-loaded feature-based structure: core for app-wide singletons like interceptors and guards, shared for dumb reusable UI, and one folder per domain feature — dependencies point inward, features never import each other. Inside a feature I split smart components that inject services from dumb OnPush components that only take inputs, which keeps tests cheap. HTTP and business logic sit in services; state starts local and only gets promoted to a store when it's genuinely shared. Cross-cutting things — auth token, error mapping — are interceptors. The thing I'd revisit is X, because Y.”
